topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA:  
Neural architecture search can discover layer-wise, epoch-segmented adaptive dropout schedules that, when combined with Monte Carlo dropout, significantly improve uncertainty calibration on clean, balanced image classification datasets relative to fixed dropout schedules, without compromising accuracy and while maintaining feasible computational cost.

BACKGROUND:  
Accurate uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks remains a critical challenge, especially for safety-sensitive applications. While Monte Carlo dropout offers a practical Bayesian approximation, fixed dropout rates limit flexibility in modeling uncertainty across training dynamics and network depth. Prior adaptive dropout methods either lack targeted optimization for calibration or incur prohibitive computational overhead, leaving open whether efficient NAS can tailor dropout schedules to produce better calibrated uncertainty estimates.

APPROACH:  
We employ differentiable neural architecture search (DARTS) to optimize dropout schedules defined as piecewise-constant dropout rates per layer across discrete training epoch segments within a ResNet-34 architecture. The search jointly optimizes for classification accuracy and Expected Calibration Error (ECE) on a clean validation set, constraining the search space to dropout rates from 0 to 0.7 in 0.1 increments and limiting epoch segments to ensure tractability. The optimized schedules are evaluated with 50 Monte Carlo forward passes at test time to assess uncertainty calibration.

EXPERIMENT:  
We evaluate on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and STL-10 datasets, measuring calibration via ECE, Negative Log Likelihood (NLL), and Brier Score, while monitoring classification accuracy. Baselines include fixed uniform dropout with MC dropout, heuristic adaptive dropout schedules, NAS-optimized schedules targeting accuracy alone, and deep ensembles. Statistical significance is assessed over multiple seeds with corrections for multiple comparisons. Computational overhead is quantified to ensure practical feasibility.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely integrates efficient differentiable NAS to discover adaptive dropout schedules explicitly optimized for uncertainty calibration, differentiating from prior adaptive dropout strategies that focus on variational formulations or accuracy alone. By constraining datasets to low-noise, balanced scenarios, we isolate calibration improvements attributable to adaptive dropout scheduling, thereby advancing principled and computationally viable uncertainty quantification in deep image classifiers.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA: Incorporating explicit feature-level uncertainty propagation throughout intermediate layers of deep neural networks significantly improves out-of-distribution uncertainty estimation compared to output-layer Bayesian methods alone.

BACKGROUND: Reliable uncertainty quantification (UQ) in deep neural networks (DNNs) is critical for safe deployment in real-world, high-stakes applications, especially under distributional shift. Current dominant approaches—such as MC Dropout and Deep Ensembles—model uncertainty primarily at the output layer, potentially overlooking how uncertainty accumulates and transforms within intermediate feature representations, which may limit robustness in detecting out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs.

APPROACH: We propose extending MC Dropout to propagate and update uncertainty distributions at multiple intermediate feature layers by independently sampling dropout masks per layer during inference and representing intermediate activations as stochastic distributions rather than fixed values. Uncertainty is quantified by aggregating multiple stochastic forward passes, enabling hierarchical epistemic uncertainty modeling that captures uncertainty accumulation across the network depth, rather than solely at output logits.

EXPERIMENT: We will train models on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 and evaluate OOD uncertainty detection on SVHN, LSUN (cropped and resized), and TinyImageNet-O, comparing our feature-level uncertainty propagation method against three matched-capacity baselines: (1) output-layer MC Dropout, (2) Deep Ensembles, and (3) output-layer Bayesian Neural Networks via variational inference. We will assess OOD detection performance using AUROC, AUPR, FPR@TPR, and calibration quality via expected calibration error (ECE) on both in-distribution and OOD data, performing multiple runs with statistical significance testing. Ablation studies will validate the contribution of each layer’s uncertainty propagation.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior works applying MC Dropout or Bayesian methods only at output layers, our approach explicitly models and propagates uncertainty distributions at intermediate features, enabling hierarchical uncertainty estimation. This provides a theoretically grounded mechanism to capture uncertainty accumulation and transformation within network hierarchies, differentiating our method from existing multi-layer dropout applications that lack explicit uncertainty distribution modeling and aggregation.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA: Incorporating normalizing flow-based feature-space density estimation into deep ensembles improves predictive uncertainty calibration and out-of-distribution detection beyond ensembles combined with Bayesian weight uncertainty methods.

BACKGROUND: Despite advances in Bayesian neural networks and ensemble methods, achieving well-calibrated predictive uncertainty—especially under distributional shifts—remains an open challenge. Existing approaches often rely solely on model weight uncertainty, which can fail to detect out-of-distribution samples effectively due to lack of explicit modeling of feature distribution density. Enhancing ensembles with feature-space density estimation offers a promising direction to address this gap.

APPROACH: We extend standard deep ensembles by training a separate normalizing flow density estimator on the penultimate layer features of each ensemble member independently, capturing the feature distribution learned by each model. During inference, these flow-based likelihood scores are combined multiplicatively with the ensemble’s predictive probabilities, yielding calibrated uncertainty estimates that explicitly leverage feature-space density for improved OOD detection. The combined prediction integrates Bayesian weight uncertainty from ensembles with explicit feature density modeling, supported by theoretical justification that density likelihoods modulate confidence in ensemble outputs.

EXPERIMENT: We evaluate on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 as in-distribution datasets using expected calibration error (ECE) and negative log-likelihood (NLL), and assess OOD detection performance on SVHN and TinyImageNet via AUROC. Baselines include standard deep ensembles, MC-dropout ensembles, and variational inference ensembles. Ablations include flows trained on random features and input-space to isolate feature-space density effects. Improvement is defined as ≥10% reduction in ECE and NLL over the best baseline, and ≥5 point AUROC gain over MC-dropout ensembles, with statistical significance tests confirming results.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior methods that apply uncertainty quantification solely through Bayesian weight uncertainty or aggregate shallow heuristics, our approach uniquely integrates explicit, independently trained normalizing flow density estimators on learned feature representations within ensembles. This fusion addresses known limitations of feature-space density estimation for OOD detection by grounding density scores in well-calibrated ensemble outputs and offers rigorous ablation studies to attribute improvements specifically to feature-space density modeling.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA: Incorporating domain-specific physical constraints as explicit, mathematically tractable priors in Bayesian neural networks significantly reduces epistemic uncertainty compared to standard Monte Carlo dropout methods in safety-critical medical image classification tasks.

BACKGROUND: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks is critical for high-stakes applications such as medical diagnosis, where overconfident but incorrect predictions can have severe consequences. While Monte Carlo (MC) dropout is widely used for estimating uncertainty, it often lacks the ability to incorporate expert domain knowledge, limiting its capacity to reduce epistemic uncertainty. There is a pressing need to develop principled methods that embed domain-specific constraints directly into Bayesian priors to improve uncertainty calibration and safety.

APPROACH: We propose to explicitly encode domain knowledge—such as lesion boundary smoothness in dermoscopy images or anatomical plausibility in chest X-rays—as hierarchical Gaussian priors shaping the weight distributions of Bayesian neural networks. These priors will be formally constructed using expert-informed spatial correlation models and anatomical atlases, integrated via constrained variational inference or Hamiltonian Monte Carlo sampling to maintain inference tractability. By comparing these domain-informed BNNs against both MC dropout BNNs and BNNs with generic priors, we isolate the effect of domain-specific priors on epistemic uncertainty.

EXPERIMENT: We will evaluate on the ISIC 2018 skin lesion and ChestX-ray14 datasets, measuring epistemic uncertainty reduction via Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and Mutual Information (MI) on both in-distribution and rigorously defined out-of-distribution (OOD) test splits. Classification accuracy and AUC will also be recorded to ensure no detrimental trade-offs. Statistical significance of uncertainty improvements will be assessed using paired t-tests across multiple training runs.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior works that rely on generic priors or solely MC dropout for uncertainty quantification, this study provides a reproducible framework to mathematically encode and validate explicit, domain-specific physical constraints as Bayesian priors in neural networks. This approach pioneers the integration of expert anatomical and morphological knowledge into scalable Bayesian inference for medical image classification, aiming for improved epistemic uncertainty calibration with clinically meaningful impact.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA: Incorporate learned data-dependent priors parameterized by hypernetworks conditioned on batch-level feature statistics into Bayesian neural networks to improve predictive uncertainty quantification across diverse image classification tasks.

BACKGROUND: Existing Bayesian neural networks typically use fixed, data-agnostic prior distributions over weights, which may limit their capacity to capture complex, dataset-specific uncertainty patterns. This can result in suboptimal calibration and poor out-of-distribution (OOD) detection performance, hindering reliable uncertainty quantification critical for safety-sensitive applications.

APPROACH: We propose extending variational Bayesian inference frameworks by introducing a hypernetwork that generates prior parameters conditioned on stabilized, aggregated batch-level feature embeddings from training data. This learned, data-dependent prior adapts dynamically to dataset complexity, enabling tighter uncertainty bounds in high-data regions and more flexible uncertainty elsewhere, while controlling for model capacity and optimization effects through rigorous ablations.

EXPERIMENT: We empirically evaluate ResNet-18 Bayesian neural networks with learned priors versus fixed Gaussian priors, Monte Carlo dropout, and deep ensembles on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and ImageNet-100. Quantitative metrics include Expected Calibration Error (ECE), Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL), Brier score, and OOD detection performance measured by predictive entropy differences, AUROC, and AUPR on CIFAR-10 vs SVHN and ImageNet-100 vs ImageNet-O. Statistical significance tests (paired t-tests, p<0.05) and controlled ablations isolate the impact of learned priors.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior hierarchical or empirical Bayes approaches, our method explicitly learns priors conditioned on data-driven summary statistics via hypernetworks, enabling a more expressive and adaptive prior formulation while maintaining Bayesian validity. We strengthen theoretical grounding on identifiability and overfitting risks and rigorously control for confounding factors, advancing principled and practical uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA:  
Integrating HfZrO2-based ferroelectric tunnel junctions (FTJs) with atomic layer deposited HfO2 memristor arrays reduces the average energy consumption per synaptic event by at least 30% compared to pure memristor synapses under identical neuromorphic workloads.

BACKGROUND:  
Energy efficiency is a critical bottleneck in neuromorphic computing hardware, where synaptic devices dominate power consumption during large-scale spiking neural network (SNN) operations. While memristor-based synapses offer compact, analog weight storage, their switching energy and variability limit further efficiency gains. Combining FTJs with memristors leverages non-volatile polarization to modulate switching thresholds, potentially lowering programming currents and overall synaptic energy.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hybrid synapse architecture by fabricating co-located FTJ-memristor stacks using precise ALD processes to integrate HfZrO2 ferroelectric layers with HfO2 memristors. Device-level electrical characterization will quantify switching thresholds, endurance, and conductance tuning. The hybrid arrays will emulate synaptic functionality in a calibrated neuromorphic platform running a two-layer STDP-trained SNN on the event-based N-MNIST dataset, allowing direct comparison of energy per synaptic event and network accuracy against pure HfO2 memristor arrays and ideal memristor baselines.

EXPERIMENT:  
Measure and compare the average energy consumption per synaptic event (in picojoules) during both training and inference phases on the N-MNIST dataset, ensuring ±1% classification accuracy parity. Employ high-precision synchronized current sensing under identical operational and computational loads to validate whether the hybrid FTJ-memristor array achieves ≥30% energy reduction over the pure memristor baseline, falsifying the hypothesis if this threshold is not met within a ±5% statistical margin.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely combines ferroelectric tunnel junctions with conventional HfO2 memristors in a scalable, CMOS-compatible hybrid synapse design, supported by a rigorous energy dissipation model and realistic system-level benchmarking on a neuromorphic workload. Unlike prior memristor-only or 2D material synapses, our approach leverages FTJ-induced switching modulation to achieve substantial, quantifiable energy savings validated under biologically relevant training protocols and datasets.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA: Integrating ferroelectric tunnel junctions (FTJs) with memristive synapses will reduce energy consumption per synaptic event by at least 30% compared to state-of-the-art atomic layer deposited HfO2-based memristors under identical operational conditions, without compromising neuromorphic task accuracy.

BACKGROUND: Energy efficiency remains a critical bottleneck in neuromorphic computing hardware, where synaptic devices consume significant power during plasticity updates. While HfO2-based memristors provide reliable switching, their energy costs limit large-scale integration. Ferroelectric tunnel junctions offer voltage-driven polarization switching with potentially lower switching energy, but their integration with memristive synapses and impact on system-level performance remain unexplored.

APPROACH: We propose fabricating hybrid FTJ-memristive synapses by incorporating a ~10 nm HfZrO2 ferroelectric tunnel barrier within the memristive stack, enabling energy-efficient modulation of synaptic conductance via voltage-controlled polarization switching. Device-level characterization will measure switching voltages, speed, endurance, and energy per synaptic event under identical pulse conditions, followed by neuromorphic benchmarking using spike-timing-dependent plasticity (STDP) implemented in crossbar arrays.

EXPERIMENT: The hypothesis will be tested by comparing hybrid FTJ-memristors against ALD HfO2 memristors on the MNIST and Neuromorphic-MNIST (N-MNIST) datasets, measuring classification accuracy, endurance, retention, switching linearity, and especially average energy consumed per synaptic event during plasticity and inference. The hypothesis is supported if the FTJ hybrid consumes ≥30% less energy per event without more than 2% absolute accuracy loss; otherwise it is falsified.

NOVELTY: This work uniquely integrates ferroelectric tunnel barriers within conventional memristive synapses to leverage polarization-driven, ultra-low-energy conductance modulation, differentiating from prior studies focused solely on either FTJs or HfO2 memristors. It rigorously addresses practical fabrication challenges and evaluates comprehensive device and system-level tradeoffs, providing clear evidence of the hybrid approach’s potential for scalable, energy-efficient neuromorphic computing.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA:  
Integrating ferroelectric tunnel junctions (FTJs) with memristive synapses can reduce device-level switching energy by ≥20% compared to conventional HfO₂/TaOx memristors, but system-level energy efficiency gains depend critically on workload characteristics and peripheral circuitry overheads, which will be quantitatively evaluated through co-designed device fabrication, circuit-level modeling, and neuromorphic benchmarking.

BACKGROUND:  
Energy efficiency at the synaptic device level is crucial for scalable neuromorphic computing, yet prior memristor-based synapses face limitations due to switching energy and variability. While FTJs offer promising low-barrier modulation mechanisms potentially reducing switching energy, their practical impact on system-level energy remains unclear because peripheral circuitry and workload-dependent activity patterns can dominate total energy costs. Addressing this gap requires rigorous, experimentally validated co-design from device to system level to quantify true energy benefits.

APPROACH:  
We will fabricate comparable synaptic arrays of (1) conventional HfO₂/TaOx memristors and (2) hybrid FTJ-memristors with an integrated ferroelectric tunneling layer under matched process conditions, performing statistically significant switching energy characterizations with standardized protocols to control device variability and parasitic effects. Device models will be integrated into a circuit simulator enhanced with silicon-calibrated peripheral blocks (write drivers, sense amplifiers, ADC/DAC) to simulate energy consumption on two established neuromorphic benchmarks—N-MNIST (moderate spiking activity) and DVS Gesture (high temporal sparsity)—allowing workload-dependent system-level energy analysis.

EXPERIMENT:  
Success is validated by demonstrating (i) a ≥20% reduction in average device-level switching energy per synaptic update for FTJ-memristors versus baseline memristors across multiple fabrication batches, and (ii) ≥10% total system-level energy savings on both N-MNIST and DVS Gesture workloads after including peripheral overheads; failure to meet either threshold falsifies the hypothesis and identifies bottlenecks for future design.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely combines experimental fabrication of hybrid FTJ-memristor synapses with co-designed, silicon-calibrated circuit-level simulations and workload-specific benchmarking to rigorously quantify the interplay of device innovations, peripheral overheads, and activity-driven energy efficiency—advancing beyond prior studies that either focus solely on device physics or isolated circuit models without integrated validation or workload contextualization.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA: Incorporating ferroelectric domain wall dynamics into memristor-based synapses enables at least a 30% reduction in energy consumption per synaptic operation compared to conventional filamentary HfO₂ memristors, without compromising classification accuracy on event-based neuromorphic tasks.

BACKGROUND: Energy efficiency remains a critical bottleneck in neuromorphic computing hardware, where conventional filamentary memristors suffer from high switching energy and variability. Ferroelectric domain wall motion offers a fundamentally different, low-energy switching mechanism that promises more uniform and reliable synaptic weight updates, yet its system-level energy benefits under realistic neuromorphic workloads remain unquantified.

APPROACH: We propose to design and evaluate spiking neural networks (SNNs) employing memristor synapses that leverage ferroelectric domain wall dynamics modeled from experimentally validated device physics. Identical SNN architectures using spike-timing-dependent plasticity (STDP) will be implemented for both ferroelectric and filamentary memristor synapses to isolate the impact of switching mechanisms on energy and accuracy.

EXPERIMENT: The comparative study will benchmark energy per synaptic operation and classification accuracy on the Neuromorphic MNIST (N-MNIST) and DVS Gesture datasets, using physics-based device simulations and/or fabricated arrays with rigorous energy measurement protocols that exclude peripheral overhead. Success requires demonstrating ≥30% energy saving with ≤1% accuracy degradation relative to HfO₂ filamentary memristor baselines fabricated or modeled under matched conditions.

NOVELTY: This work uniquely integrates domain wall-driven ferroelectric memristors within comprehensive neuromorphic system-level benchmarks, providing quantitative evidence for substantial energy savings beyond prior studies focused on device characterization or isolated synaptic functions, and addresses fabrication uniformity, endurance, and measurement rigor rarely consolidated in existing reports.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA: Integrating tunable ferroelectric HfZrO₂ layers into memristive synapses will reduce energy consumption during synaptic weight updates by at least 30% compared to conventional ALD HfO₂ memristors under identical operating conditions.

BACKGROUND: Conventional HfO₂-based memristors are promising for neuromorphic computing but suffer from relatively high programming energy, limiting large-scale, energy-efficient implementations. Achieving substantial energy savings in synaptic weight updates without compromising device reliability and functional performance remains a critical open challenge in scalable neuromorphic hardware design.

APPROACH: We propose fabricating memristive synapses that integrate a tunable ferroelectric HfZrO₂ layer compatible with CMOS processes to reduce programming voltage and current through controlled polarization switching. These devices will be benchmarked against state-of-the-art ALD HfO₂ memristors and recently reported 2D material-based synapses, using strictly matched pulse parameters and testing conditions to isolate energy efficiency gains while ensuring comparable linearity, symmetry, retention, and endurance.

EXPERIMENT: Device-level energy per synaptic weight update will be measured by integrating power consumption over programming pulses on fabricated crossbar arrays under identical environmental and electrical conditions. System-level validation will involve incorporating these synapses into a spiking neural network trained on N-MNIST and DVS Gesture datasets, evaluating energy consumption during learning and inference as well as classification accuracy, with a ≥30% energy reduction and ≤5% accuracy degradation as pass/fail criteria.

NOVELTY: This work uniquely combines tunable ferroelectric switching physics in HfZrO₂ with rigorous experimental controls and a quantitative theoretical model to demonstrate a significant, reproducible reduction in synaptic programming energy, advancing beyond prior memristive and 2D material synapses by explicitly addressing device variability, scalability, and neuromorphic system integration challenges.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA: Integrating experimentally-guided enhanced sampling molecular dynamics simulations initialized from AlphaFold2 predictions with orthogonal biophysical restraints enables quantitatively improved ensemble characterization of intrinsically disordered protein regions over static or purely data-driven models.

BACKGROUND: Intrinsically disordered protein regions (IDRs) challenge traditional structure prediction methods due to their conformational heterogeneity, which AlphaFold2 captures only as static or confidence-weighted single conformations. Current ensemble modeling approaches often rely solely on experimental data without leveraging AlphaFold’s structural priors, limiting accuracy and interpretability. A robust integration of AlphaFold predictions with physics-based sampling and multi-modal experimental restraints is needed to realistically model IDR ensembles.

APPROACH: Starting from AlphaFold2 predicted structures for curated IDPs (e.g., α-synuclein, tau, p53 transactivation domain), we will perform enhanced sampling molecular dynamics simulations (e.g., parallel-tempering metadynamics) incorporating experimental restraints from NMR chemical shifts, residual dipolar couplings, and smFRET as ensemble-averaged soft biases within a maximum entropy framework. Controls isolating contributions of AlphaFold initialization, enhanced sampling, and experimental data will be included. Ensemble convergence, physical realism, and avoidance of overfitting will be rigorously monitored.

EXPERIMENT: Using datasets from DisProt with extensive validated NMR and smFRET data, we will benchmark against (1) raw AlphaFold2 predictions, (2) enhanced sampling MD without experimental restraints, and (3) state-of-the-art NMR-driven IDP ensemble predictors (Flexible-Meccano, ENSEMBLE). Success is measured by ≥10% improvement in RMSD of predicted vs. experimental chemical shifts (<0.5 ppm target), Q-factors for RDCs (<0.3 target), and non-significant differences in smFRET distance distributions (KS test p-value >0.1). Failure to surpass these baselines across multiple metrics falsifies the hypothesis.

NOVELTY: This work critically evaluates and justifies AlphaFold2’s utility as a meaningful structural scaffold for IDPs, combining state-of-the-art physics-based sampling with multi-modal experimental constraints within a rigorous validation framework. Unlike prior approaches that treat AlphaFold or experimental data in isolation, this integration advances beyond static predictions toward physically realistic, experimentally consistent IDP ensemble models.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA: Integrating enhanced-sampling molecular dynamics simulations with AlphaFold’s static predictions through a rigorously validated re-weighting of confidence scores and ensemble variability metrics will improve modeling of proteins’ functional conformational states beyond static structure prediction.

BACKGROUND: AlphaFold2 delivers highly accurate static protein structure predictions but inherently lacks explicit modeling of protein conformational dynamics critical for function. Experimental ensemble datasets from time-resolved crystallography and NMR reveal multiple functional states that static models cannot capture, highlighting the need for methods that bridge high-accuracy prediction with dynamic conformational sampling.

APPROACH: We propose a hybrid computational pipeline that starts with AlphaFold2 predictions and applies enhanced-sampling MD simulations (e.g., accelerated MD or metadynamics) to generate conformational ensembles. These ensembles inform a novel re-weighting scheme combining AlphaFold residue-level confidence scores (pLDDT) with MD-derived structural variability metrics (e.g., RMSF), calibrated through robust structural alignment, clustering, and normalization protocols, thereby yielding ensemble predictions more reflective of functional states.

EXPERIMENT: We will benchmark against experimentally derived ensembles from time-resolved XFEL crystallography (adenylate kinase, PDB IDs 6AKE, 1AKE) and NMR ensembles from the Protein Ensemble Database (calmodulin, PED00001), comparing predicted ensembles to experimental ones using ensemble RMSD and Earth Mover’s Distance metrics focused on active sites and overall backbone. Success is defined as ≥20% reduction in eRMSD and statistically significant (p<0.05) improvement in conformational population correlations relative to static AlphaFold predictions, with negative controls including MD initiated from experimental structures and normal mode analysis.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior studies that apply MD or static AlphaFold predictions independently, our method uniquely integrates AlphaFold’s internal confidence metrics with dynamic ensemble variability into a unified, statistically validated scoring framework, explicitly addressing the interpretability and reliability of this integration, and systematically benchmarking against high-quality time-resolved experimental ensembles to rigorously quantify improvements in functional state modeling.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA: Integrate low-resolution cryo-EM density maps directly as voxelized spatial inputs and a density-fitting loss into AlphaFold’s prediction pipeline to improve multi-protein complex modeling accuracy beyond AlphaFold 3 (AF3).

BACKGROUND: While AlphaFold 3 achieves state-of-the-art accuracy in predicting multi-protein complexes using sequence information alone, it does not explicitly leverage experimental cryo-EM data despite the growing availability of low-resolution density maps for large assemblies. Existing hybrid approaches incorporate cryo-EM maps only post hoc or lack AlphaFold’s deep learning backbone, limiting their ability to resolve subunit interfaces and relative orientations accurately.

APPROACH: Modify AlphaFold’s input features to include aligned, voxelized cryo-EM density grids corresponding to target complexes, and augment the training loss with a density-fitting term that penalizes discrepancies between predicted atomic coordinates and cryo-EM densities. Fine-tune this extended AlphaFold model on a curated subset of multi-protein complexes from the Electron Microscopy Protein Complex Benchmark (EMPCB), enabling the network to learn to integrate spatial constraints from low-resolution maps during end-to-end structure prediction.

EXPERIMENT: Compare the fine-tuned model’s multi-protein complex predictions to AF3 alone, a fine-tuned AlphaFold baseline without cryo-EM input, hybrid methods like DeepTracer and CryoDRGN, and naive rigid-body fitting of AF3 structures into cryo-EM maps, using the EMPCB and a subset of Protein Data Bank complexes with paired cryo-EM and high-resolution structures. Evaluate performance quantitatively by DockQ score and interface RMSD (iRMSD), requiring a statistically significant ≥10% improvement in DockQ over AF3 on ≥70% of complexes (p < 0.05, paired t-test) to confirm hypothesis validity.

NOVELTY: This approach uniquely integrates cryo-EM density as a volumetric input and supervisory signal within AlphaFold’s end-to-end deep learning framework, addressing limitations of low-resolution ambiguity by joint sequence-structure-density learning, unlike prior methods that rely on post hoc fitting or separate modeling pipelines.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA: Integrating AlphaFold2 static predictions with selectively enhanced sampling molecular dynamics simulations guided by experimentally defined collective variables improves accurate prediction of functional protein conformational ensembles under physiological conditions.

BACKGROUND: While AlphaFold2 excels at predicting static protein structures from sequence, it often misses functionally relevant conformational heterogeneity critical for protein activity in vivo. Current approaches rarely integrate experimentally validated dynamic information or enhanced sampling tailored to key conformational transitions, limiting their ability to predict ensembles that reflect physiological functional states.

APPROACH: Starting from AlphaFold2 models, we perform selectively enhanced sampling MD simulations (e.g., Gaussian accelerated MD or metadynamics) biased along experimentally identified collective variables (CVs) linked to functional transitions—extracted via a semi-automated pipeline from literature and structural databases like DynBench. This yields dynamic conformational ensembles whose populations are quantitatively compared to experimental structures and single-molecule FRET-derived state distributions, enabling refinement beyond static predictions.

EXPERIMENT: We benchmark against a curated set of proteins with multiple experimentally resolved functional conformations from DynBench (e.g., kinases Abl/Src, GPCRs, adenylate kinase) and smFRET datasets quantifying physiological conformational populations. Metrics include ensemble RMSD distributions to known conformers and Kullback–Leibler divergence between predicted and experimental state populations, comparing (1) AlphaFold2 alone, (2) unbiased MD from AF2, and (3) AF2 plus enhanced sampling. Statistical significance of improvements (e.g., >1 Å RMSD reduction and >20% KL divergence decrease in ≥80% of proteins) will test the hypothesis; failure to improve over baselines falsifies it.

NOVELTY: This approach uniquely integrates AlphaFold2’s static predictions with targeted, experimentally guided enhanced sampling protocols and a robust framework for defining and validating CVs, moving beyond prior attempts that rely solely on static models or unguided MD. It explicitly links predicted ensemble shifts to functional assays and rigorously quantifies improvements over established baselines with multifaceted metrics and controls.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA: Integrating dynamic conformational ensemble modeling generated via enhanced sampling molecular dynamics with AlphaFold predicted structures improves accuracy in predicting protein functional states beyond static AlphaFold models alone.

BACKGROUND: While AlphaFold2 and AlphaFold-Multimer have revolutionized static protein structure prediction, they do not capture the conformational heterogeneity critical for many proteins’ functional states. Existing studies show limited correlation between AlphaFold confidence scores and functional effects such as binding affinity changes or enzymatic activity, highlighting the need to incorporate protein dynamics for improved functional predictions.

APPROACH: Starting from high-confidence AlphaFold2/AlphaFold-Multimer models, we will generate conformational ensembles using advanced enhanced sampling MD methods like Gaussian accelerated MD (GaMD) and Markov State Models (MSMs) to capture relevant substates. Representative ensemble structures obtained via clustering will be input into physics-based scoring functions and machine learning models trained on dynamic structure-function datasets (Protein Ensemble Database, ProTherm, SKEMPI 2.0) to predict functional parameters such as ΔΔG of binding and enzymatic turnover.

EXPERIMENT: We will benchmark static AlphaFold models, AlphaFold structures refined by conventional MD, and ensemble-informed predictions by comparing their correlation (Pearson’s r) and RMSE against experimentally measured functional data from PED, ProTherm, and SKEMPI 2.0. Statistical significance of improvements will be assessed using paired bootstrap tests, requiring a minimum 0.1 increase in correlation or 10% reduction in RMSE (p < 0.05) to validate the hypothesis.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that assesses AlphaFold predictions or pairs them with limited MD refinements, this study systematically integrates enhanced sampling MD to generate biologically relevant conformational ensembles and rigorously links ensemble heterogeneity to functional outcomes using large-scale dynamic structure-function datasets, providing a falsifiable framework to demonstrate the added predictive value of protein dynamics beyond static structures.

topic: Zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning
text:
IDEA: Incorporating dynamically updated task embeddings derived from online environment interactions during reinforcement learning training significantly improves zero-shot generalization to unseen tasks compared to static or contrastively learned embeddings.

BACKGROUND: Reinforcement learning agents typically struggle to generalize zero-shot to new tasks or environment variations due to overfitting to training distributions and insufficiently adaptive task representations. Existing approaches like meta-RL and contrastive representation learning rely on fixed or offline-learned embeddings that may not capture evolving task dynamics during deployment, limiting adaptability and robustness.

APPROACH: We propose an online embedding framework where a recurrent encoder (e.g., LSTM or GRU) continuously integrates recent state-action-reward trajectories into a latent task embedding vector that conditions the policy and value networks; the encoder parameters are jointly optimized end-to-end with the RL objective via truncated backpropagation through time. This dynamic embedding contrasts with static embeddings precomputed from offline environment descriptors and contrastive embeddings learned via prior methods, enabling continual adaptation to task variations encountered during training.

EXPERIMENT: We evaluate on challenging continuous control benchmarks including Meta-World ML1 and ML10 robotic manipulation suites and DeepMind Control Suite’s Walker and Cheetah with randomized physical parameters, measuring zero-shot generalization performance as normalized average episode return on held-out unseen tasks, with significance tested by paired t-tests over 10+ random seeds. Baselines include agents with static task embeddings, agents without task embeddings, and contrastive embedding agents from state-of-the-art methods, controlling embedding capacities and hyperparameters for fair comparison.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior meta-RL and context-based methods such as PEARL and VariBAD that learn fixed or slowly updated context embeddings, our approach introduces an explicitly online-updated recurrent embedding trained jointly with the policy to capture fine-grained, evolving task characteristics. Theoretical analysis grounds why online adaptation of embeddings better approximates underlying task distributions under assumptions of partial observability and trajectory sufficiency, providing formal justification missing in earlier work.

topic: Zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning
text:
IDEA:  
Reinforcement learning agents trained with dynamically generated, task-agnostic symbolic abstractions learned via neural discrete latent models will achieve significantly improved zero-shot generalization on unseen environment variations compared to agents using fixed hand-designed or task-specifically learned abstractions.

BACKGROUND:  
Zero-shot generalization remains a fundamental challenge in reinforcement learning, as agents often overfit to training environments and fail to transfer learned policies to novel scenarios. Prior approaches leveraging fixed, hand-designed symbolic state representations improve interpretability but lack adaptability, while continuous latent representation learning methods often capture task-specific features that limit out-of-distribution generalization. A principled method for learning abstract, symbolic state representations without task-specific supervision is needed to enhance policy robustness across diverse, unseen environments.

APPROACH:  
We propose an end-to-end framework where a dynamically trained, neural discrete abstraction module transforms raw observations into compact symbolic latent codes via unsupervised auxiliary objectives (e.g., observation reconstruction and future state prediction). This task-agnostic abstraction module is jointly optimized with the RL policy (using algorithms such as PPO or SAC) to promote abstractions that generalize across environment variations without relying on domain knowledge or task-specific signals. The policy acts on these learned symbolic representations, enabling better transfer to new environments.

EXPERIMENT:  
We evaluate zero-shot generalization performance on procedurally generated benchmarks with rich symbolic structure and visual variability, specifically MiniGrid (Chevalier-Boisvert et al., 2018) and Procgen Benchmark (Cobbe et al., 2020). We compare three agent variants: (1) a pixel-baseline trained on raw observations, (2) a fixed hand-designed symbolic abstraction baseline encoding environment-specific object states, (3) a learned task-agnostic symbolic abstraction agent as proposed. Additional baselines include task-supervised learned abstractions and continuous latent representations. Success is measured as average episodic completion rate on held-out, unseen environment configurations over multiple seeds, with statistical significance analyzed via power-analyzed t-tests. The hypothesis is falsified if the learned symbolic abstraction agent fails to achieve at least a 15% absolute improvement over the fixed abstraction baseline or shows no statistically significant advantage.

NOVELTY:  
This work rigorously formalizes and operationalizes “symbolic” abstractions as discrete, task-agnostic latent representations learned via neural bottlenecks with auxiliary unsupervised objectives, advancing beyond prior hand-designed or task-supervised abstractions. By jointly training abstraction and policy end-to-end without task-specific engineering, it offers a scalable, theoretically motivated framework that explicitly targets zero-shot generalization, distinguishing it from previous representation learning and symbolic RL methods.

topic: Zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning
text:
IDEA:  
Reinforcement learning agents that explicitly learn and leverage causal structure from environment dynamics via scalable causal discovery and graph-based policy conditioning achieve significantly improved zero-shot generalization to unseen task variants compared to agents relying solely on correlation-based or contrastive representations.

BACKGROUND:  
Zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning remains a fundamental challenge, as agents trained on specific environment variants often fail to transfer robustly to unseen tasks exhibiting novel dynamics or interventions. Prior methods primarily use correlation-based or contrastive representation learning but lack explicit modeling of underlying causal mechanisms governing environment transitions, limiting their robustness under domain shifts. Incorporating causal structure learning offers a promising inductive bias to disentangle cause-effect relations from spurious correlations, potentially enabling better transfer across out-of-distribution tasks.

APPROACH:  
We propose augmenting a model-free RL agent (e.g., PPO) with a self-supervised causal discovery module adapted for high-dimensional, partially observed RL states, which infers sparse causal graphs over state variables using continuous optimization of structural equation models (e.g., an RL-tailored adaptation of NOTEARS). The discovered causal graph conditions policy decisions via a graph neural network embedding that explicitly uses inferred causal parents rather than raw states, trained jointly through an interleaved procedure combining trajectory collection, causal graph learning, and policy updates. We incorporate dimensionality reduction and disentangled representation learning prior to causal discovery to handle noise and complexity, and rigorously control for model capacity and inductive biases by employing matched GNN baselines without causal inference.

EXPERIMENT:  
We evaluate zero-shot generalization on held-out environment variants from the Procgen Benchmark and Meta-World robotic manipulation suite, measuring average episodic return and generalization gap relative to training performance. We compare four agent types—(1) standard PPO with CNNs, (2) PPO with contrastive representation learning (e.g., CURL), (3) the proposed causal structure learning agent, and (4) an ablated causal agent without causal inference but identical architecture. Statistical significance is tested via paired t-tests over multiple seeds and environment variants, requiring causal agents to outperform baselines by ≥15% in return with ≥25% smaller generalization gaps (p < 0.01) to support the hypothesis.

NOVELTY:  
This work meaningfully advances zero-shot RL generalization by integrating scalable, robust causal discovery methods tailored for high-dimensional RL states with joint policy training, unlike prior approaches that rely on correlation-based or contrastive representations alone. By explicitly disentangling causal relations in environment dynamics and rigorously controlling for architectural confounds, it provides the first comprehensive empirical demonstration of causal structure learning's concrete benefits for zero-shot transfer in complex procedural and robotic benchmark suites.

topic: Zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning
text:
IDEA:  
Zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning improves significantly when policies are trained using multi-modal sensory inputs (proprioception, vision, and audio) with modality-specific encoders and attention-based fusion, even without data augmentation or explicit regularization.

BACKGROUND:  
Reinforcement learning (RL) agents often fail to generalize zero-shot to out-of-distribution environments, limiting their deployment in complex real-world tasks. Prior approaches focus heavily on data augmentation or representation learning to improve generalization but rarely isolate the intrinsic benefits of multi-modal sensory inputs. Understanding whether and how combining complementary sensory modalities alone enhances zero-shot robustness remains an open problem.

APPROACH:  
We propose a multi-headed encoder architecture where each modality—proprioception (1D CNN), vision (ResNet-18), and audio (1D CNN + LSTM)—is processed separately and then dynamically fused with an attention-based module before policy learning via PPO. Crucially, no data augmentation or additional regularization is employed to isolate the impact of multi-modal perception. A realistically synchronized audio simulation pipeline is integrated to ensure meaningful and consistent audio inputs aligned with proprioceptive and visual signals.

EXPERIMENT:  
We evaluate zero-shot generalization on DeepMind Control Suite locomotion tasks (Humanoid, Quadruped) augmented with synchronized proprioceptive, visual, and audio inputs, and on the Meta-World ML45 manipulation benchmark enhanced with synthesized auditory cues from object interactions. Metrics include normalized average return on held-out test environments with differing terrains, lighting, and object properties, statistical significance testing over 20 seeds, sample efficiency, and robustness (variance and failure rates). Controls include single-modality baselines using identical encoder architectures without fusion, multi-modal concatenation baselines, and state-of-the-art multi-modal RL methods (e.g., SMiRL, CURL adapted for multi-modal inputs).

NOVELTY:  
This work rigorously isolates the effect of multi-modal sensory inputs on zero-shot generalization by controlling for architectural improvements and excluding data augmentation, supported by a validated realistic audio simulation pipeline rarely used in RL benchmarks. Unlike prior studies that conflate multi-modality benefits with augmentation or offline RL techniques, this study provides the first comprehensive, statistically robust evidence that multi-modal perception alone can significantly enhance zero-shot generalization in RL.

topic: Zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning
text:
IDEA:  
Incorporating online differentiable causal structure learning into reinforcement learning agents improves zero-shot generalization to out-of-distribution environments by enabling policy invariance to spurious correlations and interventions on causal mechanisms.

BACKGROUND:  
Reinforcement learning (RL) agents often fail to generalize zero-shot to environments where underlying causal mechanisms differ, limiting robustness in real-world applications. Prior approaches relying on observational data or representation learning have struggled to disentangle causal from spurious correlations, resulting in poor out-of-distribution (OOD) performance when environment dynamics or reward functions change. Addressing causal identifiability and stability in RL remains an open challenge for improving true generalization.

APPROACH:  
We integrate a differentiable online causal discovery module (e.g., an adapted NOTEARS algorithm) that learns and updates a causal graph over latent state features during training with interventional data gathered from environment interactions. The policy network conditions on causally filtered latent representations, enabling counterfactual reasoning and causal invariance regularization that guide policy updates to focus on stable causal factors rather than spurious correlations.

EXPERIMENT:  
We evaluate zero-shot generalization on Procgen (CoinRun, Maze) and the DeepMind Control Suite with systematically designed OOD test environments generated via explicit interventions on causal factors (e.g., physics parameters, object interactions). Baselines include PPO, CURL-based self-supervised RL, and IRM-adapted RL agents. Success is defined as achieving at least 15% relative improvement in average episodic return over the best baseline on held-out OOD environments, with rigorous ablations isolating the causal module’s contribution and statistical tests (paired t-tests, p<0.05) for validation.

NOVELTY:  
This work pioneers scalable, stable online causal discovery integrated directly into RL pipelines for zero-shot generalization, explicitly leveraging interventional data and counterfactual reasoning for policy invariance—advancing beyond prior methods limited to observational invariance or offline causal inference in RL.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA:  
Under combined constraints of limited bandwidth and noisy communication channels, multi-agent systems trained in referential games will spontaneously develop emergent communication protocols exhibiting measurable error-correcting properties analogous to redundancy in human language.

BACKGROUND:  
Multi-agent emergent communication often assumes idealized, noise-free channels, overlooking realistic constraints such as bandwidth limits and channel noise that challenge reliable information transfer. Understanding whether and how agents naturally develop error-correcting strategies under such constraints remains an open problem with implications for both communication theory and interpretable AI. Existing work rarely disentangles the effects of noise and bandwidth on emergent protocol structure or rigorously quantifies genuine error correction beyond task success rates.

APPROACH:  
We train sender-receiver pairs modeled as deep recurrent neural networks on a modified Emergent Communication with Referential Games (ECRG) dataset augmented with controlled channel noise (0%, 10%, 20%) and explicit message length constraints (max 5 discrete symbols). Training uses curriculum learning from noise-free to noisy channels, and includes critical control conditions with noisy channels but unconstrained bandwidth. We incorporate inductive biases and auxiliary reconstruction losses to stabilize learning and encourage robust, meaningful protocols. Post-training, we apply systematic perturbation-response analyses and classical coding-theoretic metrics (e.g., symbol entropy, mutual information, and empirical error-correction rates) to rigorously identify genuine error-correcting features in the emergent languages.

EXPERIMENT:  
We evaluate agents on held-out ECRG referential tasks under varying noise and bandwidth conditions, comparing (i) unconstrained noise-free agents, (ii) bandwidth-constrained noise-free agents, (iii) bandwidth-constrained noisy agents, and (iv) noisy unconstrained bandwidth agents, as well as against heuristic baselines combining Huffman coding and classical error-correcting codes. Success is measured by task accuracy under noise, redundancy metrics, and perturbation-resilience tests that reveal systematic error correction beyond baseline compression.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely integrates rigorous, task-agnostic error-correction diagnostics and critical channel control conditions to disentangle noise and bandwidth effects on emergent protocol structure. It advances beyond prior emergent communication studies by combining practical induction of error-correcting codes with interpretable semantic content grounding, while clarifying the analogy to human language redundancy via explicit information-theoretic metrics rather than informal comparisons.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA: Multi-agent systems equipped with heterogeneous sensory modalities develop more robust and generalizable emergent communication protocols than homogeneous systems under controlled architectural parity and identical training conditions.

BACKGROUND: Robust and generalizable emergent communication in multi-agent systems (MAS) remains a key challenge, particularly in environments with partial observability and diverse sensory inputs. Prior work typically focuses on homogeneous sensory settings or asymmetric observations without isolating the effects of sensory heterogeneity on communication protocol development. Understanding how diverse sensory modalities influence emergent communication can enable MAS to better coordinate in complex, real-world scenarios.

APPROACH: We propose training MAS with heterogeneous sensory inputs—combinations of vision, proprioception, and audio—versus homogeneous sensory inputs using the Multi-Agent Particle Environment extended by the rigorously validated MPE-MultiSense dataset, ensuring matched model capacity, encoder complexity, and communication bandwidth across conditions. Agents employ modality-specific encoders feeding into a shared, differentiable communication bottleneck with discrete token generation via Gumbel-softmax, trained end-to-end using MADDPG augmented with communication, alongside ablation studies and matched-capacity baselines to isolate sensory heterogeneity effects.

EXPERIMENT: We evaluate on cooperative navigation and target localization tasks measuring (1) task success rate across training and novel zero-shot test environments with unseen sensory modality combinations and agent counts, and (2) communication generalization scores defined by sustained task performance without retraining communication protocols. Statistical significance (p < 0.05) is established over multiple random seeds using t-tests, comparing heterogeneous MAS against homogeneous MAS baselines including no-communication and obverter algorithm variants.

NOVELTY: This work rigorously isolates the impact of sensory heterogeneity on emergent communication by controlling architectural confounds and introducing precise generalization metrics on the novel MPE-MultiSense dataset, advancing beyond prior studies on partial observability and asymmetric inputs by providing a formalized theoretical grounding and empirical evidence that sensory diversity intrinsically enhances communication robustness and generalization in MAS.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA: Multi-agent systems equipped with heterogeneous sensory modalities develop more efficient and robust emergent communication protocols than homogeneous sensor systems under noisy conditions, as demonstrated by higher communication success rates and faster task completion in cooperative navigation tasks.

BACKGROUND: Robust emergent communication in multi-agent systems (MAS) remains a critical challenge, particularly when agents operate under noisy sensory and communication environments. While most existing work assumes homogeneous sensor modalities, it is unclear whether and how sensor heterogeneity—mimicking real-world multi-modal perception—can enhance the efficiency and resilience of emergent protocols. Addressing this gap will inform the design of MAS for complex, real-world applications requiring adaptive, noise-resilient communication.

APPROACH: We propose a multi-agent reinforcement learning framework in the Multi-Agent Particle Environment (MPE) extended with the Multi-Modal Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MM-MARL) dataset, where agents perceive the environment through distinct modality-specific encoders (e.g., convolutional networks for vision, recurrent networks for audio, and feedforward layers for proprioception). These modality embeddings are fused into a shared latent space to generate discrete communication messages learned via Differentiable Inter-Agent Learning (DIAL) and obverter algorithms. Agents jointly optimize task policies and communication under controlled additive Gaussian noise applied to both sensor inputs and communication channels, using a noise-robust composite loss balancing cross-entropy communication error and task reward.

EXPERIMENT: We evaluate and compare (1) homogeneous sensor MAS trained with DIAL and obverter protocols, (2) heterogeneous sensor MAS trained with the same protocols, and (3) heterogeneous MAS with learned but unstructured communication baselines, all under identical noise conditions. Metrics include communication success rate—operationalized as the proportion of messages that lead to receiver actions correctly aligned with sender intent in sequential navigation—and task completion time measured by average steps to goal. Statistical rigor is ensured through multiple independent training runs, bootstrap confidence intervals, and appropriate corrections for multiple hypothesis testing. The hypothesis is supported if heterogeneous MAS outperform homogeneous baselines by at least 15% in communication success and 20% in task efficiency, with significance p < 0.05.

NOVELTY: This work explicitly isolates and quantifies the impact of sensor modality heterogeneity on emergent communication efficiency and robustness under noise, using strong learned communication baselines and carefully designed multi-modal encoders and fusion. Unlike prior studies focusing on sensor or channel optimization alone, we theoretically ground and empirically validate how multi-modal perception facilitates richer, more aligned communication protocols in MAS, advancing practical insights for deploying robust cooperative agents.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA:  
Multi-agent systems that integrate a hierarchical reinforcement learning meta-controller to dynamically evolve communication protocols based on local environmental feedback develop more efficient, robust, and adaptive emergent languages under noisy conditions than systems using fixed or statically learned protocols.

BACKGROUND:  
Current multi-agent communication frameworks often rely on either fixed protocols or learned but static communication structures, limiting adaptability and robustness in realistic, noisy environments. The challenge remains to design multi-agent systems capable of autonomously and continuously adapting their communication strategies in response to local, time-varying environmental feedback signals—such as channel noise or task progress—to improve coordination efficiency and resilience. Addressing this gap is critical for advancing scalable, noise-robust cooperative AI, especially in partially observable and complex domains.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hierarchical multi-agent reinforcement learning framework wherein a meta-controller dynamically modulates communication protocol parameters (e.g., vocabulary size, message redundancy, compositionality) based on real-time local environmental feedback, such as noise estimates and task state signals. This high-level policy guides a low-level message encoder-decoder, enabling continuous on-the-fly adaptation of the emergent language. Agents jointly optimize task performance and communication efficiency, training under stochastic communication noise and environmental perturbations to foster robust protocol evolution.

EXPERIMENT:  
We evaluate the approach on the Multi-Agent Particle Environment’s Cooperative Navigation task augmented with Gaussian communication noise (SNR levels ranging from 5 to 20 dB) and stochastic landmark perturbations, alongside the complex, partially observable RoboCup Rescue Simulation dataset. Baselines include: (1) Fixed Protocol MAS with static vocabularies, (2) Learned Fixed Protocol MAS trained with RL but without protocol adaptation, and (3) No-noise/no-feedback controls. Metrics assess task success rate, bits transmitted per successful episode, and degradation slope of success rate against noise, with rigorous statistical tests ensuring significance.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior adaptive communication methods that typically fix protocol structure during execution, our approach introduces a principled, hierarchical meta-learning mechanism for continuous protocol evolution driven solely by local environmental feedback signals. We formalize a theoretical framework quantifying emergent language efficiency and robustness under noise, providing the first empirical demonstration that dynamic protocol adaptation confers statistically significant advantages in multi-agent coordination tasks across both synthetic and realistic datasets.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA:  
Multi-agent systems with dynamically evolving communication protocols that explicitly incorporate real-time environmental feedback about channel noise achieve significantly higher long-term cooperation success in noisy, partially observable tasks compared to fixed or non-feedback adaptive protocols.

BACKGROUND:  
Effective communication under noisy and variable environmental conditions remains a critical challenge in multi-agent reinforcement learning, where fixed communication protocols limit coordination robustness and prior adaptive approaches typically lack explicit conditioning on environmental feedback. This gap constrains emergent communication effectiveness in real-world scenarios characterized by non-stationary communication channels and partial observability. Addressing how environmental feedback can be integrated to enable dynamically evolving protocols is essential for advancing robust multi-agent cooperation.

APPROACH:  
We propose end-to-end training of multi-agent reinforcement learning agents using a differentiable communication channel model that simulates realistic wireless noise derived from the DeepMIMO dataset, enabling agents to adapt their communication encoding, error correction, and retransmission strategies based on explicit environmental feedback signals (e.g., signal quality metrics). Agents’ policy networks condition message formation on both internal states and observed channel conditions, allowing dynamic evolution of communication protocols through policy gradient optimization (e.g., MAPPO). This feedback loop is formally modeled as a partially observable stochastic game with communication constraints to theoretically characterize conditions for protocol adaptability benefits.

EXPERIMENT:  
We evaluate three systems—(1) fixed-protocol MAS with static discrete messages (CommNet/VDN), (2) adaptive protocols without environmental feedback, and (3) adaptive protocols with explicit environmental feedback—on the StarCraft II Multi-Agent Challenge (SMAC) extended with DeepMIMO-modeled channel noise across multiple noise regimes. Metrics include average team win rate, variance, communication overhead, and sample efficiency measured over 1000 test episodes per scenario with statistical significance assessed over multiple independent runs. The hypothesis is confirmed if feedback-adaptive protocols outperform fixed protocols by ≥10 percentage points in win rate under high noise (>30% packet error), while maintaining comparable performance in low-noise settings; falsification occurs if fixed protocols match or exceed feedback-adaptive performance within 5 percentage points consistently.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior emergent communication and adaptive protocol works that either ignore environmental feedback or treat adaptation implicitly, this research explicitly integrates and formalizes real-time environmental feedback in communication policy evolution within a rigorously controlled MARL framework grounded in empirical wireless channel models. This approach bridges communication theory and multi-agent learning, enabling principled exploration of feedback-conditioned protocol advantages and their theoretical underpinnings in noisy, long-term cooperative tasks.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA:  
Incorporating domain-informed self-supervised representation learning tailored to raw multivariate time series improves the identifiability and generalization of causal discovery algorithms, leading to more accurate reconstruction of true causal structures than methods relying solely on hand-crafted features or generic embeddings.

BACKGROUND:  
Causal discovery from high-dimensional, nonlinear observational time series remains challenging due to complex dependencies, limited samples, latent confounding, and nonstationarity. Existing methods typically operate on raw or hand-engineered features without leveraging domain-specific inductive biases in representation learning, limiting identifiability and robustness. There is a critical gap in integrating theoretically grounded, domain-informed self-supervised learning with causal discovery to enhance causal graph recovery.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hybrid framework that first applies self-supervised representation learning on raw time series using domain-tailored augmentations and objectives (e.g., masked reconstruction with physiologically or physically plausible perturbations, contrastive predictive coding constrained by known variable co-evolution) to produce disentangled, causally relevant latent embeddings. These learned embeddings serve as inputs to downstream nonlinear causal discovery methods (e.g., conditional independence tests or neural network-based causal graph learners) with additional temporal smoothness and sparsity regularizers reflecting plausible causal dynamics. We explicitly specify the inductive biases and identifiability conditions, linking our approach to causal representation learning theory and mitigating confounders and nonstationarity through modeling assumptions.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate on three benchmark datasets with established ground truth causal structures: (1) PhysioNet Challenge 2012 clinical multivariate time series, (2) Climate Sense CMIP6 nonlinear atmospheric time series, and (3) an extended Sachs et al. (2005) flow cytometry dataset augmented with synthetic interventional time series. Comparisons include baselines applying causal discovery on raw data, generic self-supervised embeddings without domain knowledge, and state-of-the-art methods: PCMCI+, TCDF, and Granger causality. Performance is quantified by Structural Hamming Distance (SHD), True Positive Rate (TPR), False Discovery Rate (FDR), and precision-recall AUC. Success requires statistically significant improvements (≥10-15% SHD reduction and ≥10% TPR increase at comparable FDR) across multiple datasets, with ablation studies isolating domain-informed components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work, this approach explicitly integrates theoretically motivated domain-specific inductive biases into self-supervised representation learning to enhance causal identifiability in nonlinear, high-dimensional time series. It systematically isolates the impact of domain knowledge on representation quality and causal recovery, addressing confounding and nonstationarity while improving interpretability and generalizability beyond purely hand-crafted or generic feature-based causal discovery methods.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA: Incorporating rigorously extracted and validated domain-specific causal constraints into neural network-based causal discovery frameworks significantly reduces false positive causal links and improves overall causal graph accuracy in high-dimensional nonlinear time series data compared to purely data-driven methods.

BACKGROUND: Causal discovery from observational time series data, especially in high-dimensional nonlinear settings like physiological or cellular systems, remains challenging due to confounding, nonlinear dependencies, and limited samples. Existing approaches relying solely on data-driven conditional independence tests or neural architectures often yield high false positive rates, limiting interpretability and practical utility. Leveraging expert knowledge as causal constraints offers promise but requires systematic, quantitative incorporation and theoretical grounding to ensure improved causal identifiability and robustness.

APPROACH: We extend the Temporal Causal Discovery Framework (TCDF) by integrating domain-specific causal constraints derived from expert knowledge through a reproducible pipeline that encodes known impossible and mandatory edges as hard or soft constraints in the model’s adjacency structure. This is achieved via constraint-regularized loss terms and adjacency projection during training, with ablations exploring constraint strength and uncertainty handling. We develop quantitative validation protocols for expert constraints, including controls with randomized and partial constraints, and provide a theoretical framework analyzing how such constraints enhance causal identifiability and reduce false positives under realistic assumptions.

EXPERIMENT: We evaluate on (1) the PhysioNet Challenge 2012 ICU multivariate nonlinear physiological time series with expert-annotated causal relations in cardiopulmonary dynamics, and (2) a synthetic nonlinear time series extension of the Sachs et al. (2005) single-cell phosphoproteomic dataset using its well-characterized signaling pathways as ground truth. Baselines include TCDF without constraints, Runge et al. (2019)’s nonlinear conditional independence method, and a neural causal model trained without expert constraints. Performance is measured by false positive rate, F1-score, false negative rate, and robustness metrics accounting for temporal lags and latent confounding across 5-fold cross-validation and repeated random splits, with falsification defined as failure to achieve at least 20% FPR reduction and 10% F1 improvement versus all baselines without degradation beyond 5%.

NOVELTY: This work rigorously operationalizes domain expertise into causal discovery models via validated constraint extraction and integration methods with theoretical justification, going beyond prior purely data-driven or heuristic constraint uses. The combined empirical and theoretical treatment, along with comprehensive ablations against randomized control constraints, uniquely establishes when and how expert knowledge concretely improves neural causal discovery in complex nonlinear time series.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA:  
Integrating contrastive self-supervised learning with causal discovery algorithms on observational time series enhances latent confounder detection by learning invariant, causally informative embeddings that improve identifiability beyond standard conditional independence tests.

BACKGROUND:  
Latent confounders obscure causal inference from observational time series by inducing spurious dependencies that are difficult to detect using traditional conditional independence tests alone. Existing causal discovery methods, including nonlinear time series approaches like PCMCI+ and deep learning frameworks like TCDF, often lack explicit mechanisms to uncover hidden confounders, limiting faithful causal graph recovery. Bridging representation learning and causal discovery to improve confounder detection remains an open challenge.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hybrid framework that alternates between contrastive self-supervised learning (SSL) of temporal embeddings—which emphasize invariance to irrelevant perturbations and sensitivity to causal dynamics—and causal graph estimation via conditional independence tests on the learned embeddings. By training a temporal contrastive loss inspired by Contrastive Predictive Coding (CPC), our method captures latent common factors as structured embedding features, which are then integrated into PCMCI+’s conditional independence testing to reveal hidden confounding. Joint optimization with convergence criteria ensures stable learning and effective embedding-graph synergy.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluation will be conducted on benchmark datasets with established latent confounders and ground-truth causal structure: the Sachs et al. (2005) flow cytometry time series and CauseMe Challenge 2020 datasets (#9 and #10). Metrics include latent confounder detection precision, recall, F1-score, and Structural Hamming Distance (SHD) comparing inferred to true causal graphs. Baselines comprise PCMCI+, TCDF, and FCI, alongside ablations using non-contrastive embeddings and SSL-only models. A statistically significant (>10% absolute) improvement in confounder F1-score across datasets will validate the hypothesis; failure to surpass baseline variance (±5%) or degradation in SHD will falsify it.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely combines temporal contrastive self-supervised learning with classical and nonlinear conditional independence–based causal discovery to enhance latent confounder identifiability, addressing key theoretical challenges in causal representation learning. Unlike prior deep learning causal discovery methods that ignore latent confounders or treat representation learning and causal inference separately, our integrated, jointly optimized framework explicitly targets confounder detection, supported by rigorous ablation and stability analyses.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA:  
Integrating contrastive self-supervised learning representations with causal discovery algorithms on observational time series significantly improves latent confounder identification compared to existing nonlinear conditional independence and deep learning causal discovery methods.

BACKGROUND:  
Identifying latent confounders from high-dimensional, nonlinear observational time series remains a critical challenge for accurate causal inference in domains like healthcare and climate science. Existing causal discovery methods, including kernel-based conditional independence tests and deep learning frameworks, often fail to robustly capture hidden confounding factors due to limited latent feature representation and model assumptions. Enhancing latent confounder detection would improve causal graph accuracy and downstream decision-making based on observational data alone.

APPROACH:  
We propose to first learn robust latent representations of time series data using contrastive self-supervised learning (e.g., Time-series Contrastive Predictive Coding) designed to capture invariant and predictive features potentially encoding latent confounders. These learned embeddings are then input into causal discovery algorithms adapted for time series with latent confounders, such as the Fast Causal Inference (FCI) algorithm extended to temporal data. This integration explicitly leverages the improved feature space to enhance detection and modeling of latent confounding structures.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate on two real-world datasets with known latent confounders—MIMIC-III ICU clinical time series and NOAA Climate Data Records—and two synthetic datasets from the Causal Discovery Toolbox simulating nonlinear dynamical systems with controllable latent confounders. Baselines include Runge et al. (2019) nonlinear kernel-based conditional independence tests and Shajarisales et al. (2019) Temporal Causal Discovery Framework (TCDF). Metrics will focus on latent confounder identification accuracy via Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC) and causal graph quality via Structural Hamming Distance (SHD), supplemented by permutation tests, ablation studies (e.g., with/without SSL embeddings), and sensitivity analyses. Success requires ≥15% relative AUPRC improvement and ≥10% SHD reduction over baselines on synthetic data, with consistent, statistically significant domain-consistent improvements on real data.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that applies causal discovery directly on raw or shallowly processed time series, this method rigorously integrates contrastive self-supervised representation learning with causal inference to explicitly enhance latent confounder capture. This approach is theoretically grounded with clear assumptions about identifiability and temporal dynamics, validated through comprehensive ablations and controlled synthetic benchmarks, thus advancing causal representation learning beyond standard nonlinear independence testing and deep causal discovery frameworks without latent confounder modeling.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA: Incorporating adversarially learned cross-domain invariant representations into nonlinear causal discovery algorithms improves identification of causal directions under hidden confounding, outperforming state-of-the-art nonlinear conditional independence methods.

BACKGROUND: Causal discovery from purely observational data with hidden confounders remains a major challenge, especially in nonlinear, high-dimensional, or time-series settings. Existing nonlinear conditional independence (CI) methods like PCMCI+ or TCDF struggle to robustly infer causal directions when hidden confounders bias observed statistical dependencies. Leveraging domain shifts and invariant causal mechanisms offers a promising but underexplored avenue to mitigate confounding bias.

APPROACH: We propose a pipeline that first learns latent representations invariant across multiple related but distinct domains—defined rigorously as differing parameter regimes, interventions, or batch effects—via a Variational Autoencoder with an adversarial domain discriminator enforcing invariance. These invariant embeddings serve as inputs to nonlinear CI tests within causal discovery algorithms such as PCMCI+ adapted for the latent space, with the theoretical justification that domain invariance preserves causal mechanisms and reduces confounding bias. We will theoretically analyze assumptions on domain shifts ensuring identifiability under confounding and empirically verify domain relevance and invariance.

EXPERIMENT: We evaluate on the CauseMe 2023 synthetic benchmark suite with nonlinear time series and hidden confounders and the real-world Sachs et al.
