(2005) protein-signaling dataset with known ground-truth causality and hidden confounding. Performance is measured by Structural Hamming Distance (SHD) and directional AUPRC against baselines including PCMCI+ on raw features, TCDF, Runge et al. (2019) nonlinear CI-based methods, and ablation variants without domain invariance or adversarial training. Statistical significance is assessed over multiple runs with hyperparameter tuning and explicit control for confounder complexity.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior methods that apply nonlinear CI tests directly to raw or learned features without domain invariance, our approach explicitly integrates adversarially learned domain-invariant representations grounded in theoretical identifiability conditions under hidden confounding. This principled combination of invariant representation learning with nonlinear causal discovery addresses confounding in a way not previously demonstrated on realistic nonlinear datasets with rigorous evaluation and ablation studies.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA: Incorporating adaptive, context-aware metabolic pathway constraints augmented with probabilistic toxicity predictions into transformer-based generative models significantly reduces the predicted toxic metabolic byproducts of generated molecules without compromising drug-likeness.

BACKGROUND: Current generative AI models for drug discovery often neglect metabolic transformations and their toxicological consequences, leading to candidates with undesirable metabolic liabilities. Existing metabolic constraints tend to be static and heuristic-based, lacking integration of biochemical context such as tissue-specific enzyme expression and probabilistic toxicity data for metabolites, limiting the realism and safety of generated compounds.

APPROACH: We extend a transformer-based generative framework by integrating a dynamic metabolic pathway module that predicts likely metabolic transformations conditioned on biochemical context using MetaQSAR-derived reaction templates and a graph neural network. Probabilistic toxicity predictions for metabolites, obtained from a multitask deep neural network trained on Tox21 endpoints and validated on external metabolite toxicity datasets, guide adaptive reinforcement learning rewards to steer molecule generation away from toxic metabolic byproducts while preserving drug-likeness.

EXPERIMENT: We conduct comparative in silico evaluations on 1,000 molecules generated per model using ChEMBL 30 for training generation models, MetaQSAR for metabolic reaction context, and Tox21 for toxicity prediction; metrics include average weighted metabolic toxicity scores, QED, and synthetic accessibility scores, with statistical significance tested via paired t-tests. Baselines include (1) an unconstrained REINVENT 4 model, (2) a model with static MetaQSAR-derived metabolic constraints, and (3) the proposed adaptive context-aware metabolic constraint model with integrated toxicity prediction.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior approaches that employ static or heuristic metabolic filtering, this work introduces a rigorously validated, context-aware metabolic prediction coupled with probabilistic metabolite toxicity assessment to create an adaptive, closed-loop constraint within molecule generation—thus directly optimizing for safer metabolic profiles in silico while maintaining chemical diversity and drug-likeness.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA:  
Integrating multi-modal biological data—including gene expression, protein structure embeddings, and metabolomics profiles—into a unified generative AI framework will yield drug candidates with significantly higher in vitro efficacy than models trained solely on molecular structure data.

BACKGROUND:  
Current generative AI models for drug discovery predominantly rely on molecular structure representations (e.g., SMILES or graphs), which limits their ability to capture complex biological context influencing drug-target interactions and pharmacodynamics. Integrating multi-modal biological data can provide richer, complementary information reflecting cellular responses, target conformations, and metabolic environment, addressing a critical gap in designing more efficacious and biologically relevant drug candidates.

APPROACH:  
We propose a multi-encoder Transformer-based generative model architecture that separately encodes molecular graphs, gene expression perturbation profiles (LINCS L1000), protein structure embeddings (AlphaFold structures processed via ProtTrans/ESM), and metabolomics features (HMDB pathway-linked metabolites). These modalities are fused via cross-attention layers in a conditional autoregressive decoder generating novel molecules as SMILES strings. Reinforcement learning with QSAR or docking-based oracle models will bias generation toward high predicted bioactivity, with intermediate ablation studies isolating each modality’s contribution.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will train and evaluate two models: (1) a baseline SMILES-based Transformer trained only on ChEMBL v29 molecular structures, and (2) our multi-modal model integrating all four data types aligned via drug-target and pathway annotations. Generated molecules (targeting kinase families) will be synthesized or procured in batches of ≥200 each and tested in vitro for inhibition potency (IC50 <1 µM). Success is defined as a ≥20 percentage point increase in hit rate over baseline (statistically significant with p < 0.05), alongside assessments of novelty (Tanimoto similarity <0.4), synthetic accessibility, and ADMET profiles. Data preprocessing will include batch correction and missing data imputation, with contingency plans for annotation noise.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior generative drug design approaches that focus solely on molecular structure or incorporate limited biological signals indirectly, this work explicitly integrates multiple orthogonal biological data modalities with a principled fusion architecture and quantitative in vitro validation. This comprehensive multi-modal integration and rigorous ablation under controlled experimental conditions provide mechanistic insights and a novel, scalable framework for biologically informed de novo drug design.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA:  
Integrating a rigorously curated indigenous medicinal plant metabolomics dataset as an auxiliary training resource in transformer-based generative models enhances molecular diversity and scaffold novelty while preserving predicted in silico binding affinity, compared to models trained solely on large global chemical databases.

BACKGROUND:  
Current generative AI frameworks for drug discovery predominantly rely on large, global chemical libraries such as ZINC15, which may underrepresent unique chemotypes present in indigenous medicinal plants. This limits exploration of novel chemical space and potentially overlooks bioactive scaffolds with ethnopharmacological relevance. Addressing this gap requires integrating complementary natural product data while ensuring functional relevance and avoiding model degradation.

APPROACH:  
We propose training three SMILES-based transformer models: (1) a baseline on ZINC15 alone, (2) an augmented model trained jointly on ZINC15 plus a curated, canonicalized indigenous metabolomics dataset derived from AfroDB and Natural Product Atlas, and (3) a fine-tuned model pretrained on ZINC15 then adapted to the indigenous dataset with domain adaptation techniques to mitigate catastrophic forgetting. We will implement regularization, early stopping, and balanced sampling strategies to maintain performance across chemical domains.

EXPERIMENT:  
Models will be evaluated by generating 10,000 molecules each via nucleus sampling (top-p=0.9), measuring molecular diversity (Shannon entropy of Morgan fingerprints, Tanimoto similarity distributions), scaffold novelty (percentage of novel Bemis-Murcko scaffolds against ZINC15 training), and predicted binding affinity (via retrospective benchmarking and ML/docking scores on three biologically relevant targets linked to indigenous plant pharmacology). Significant increases of >10% in diversity and >15% scaffold novelty without >5% loss in predicted affinity (p<0.05) will validate the hypothesis; failure to achieve these metrics or degradation in fine-tuned model performance will falsify it.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely combines rigorous domain adaptation and validation protocols to integrate curated indigenous metabolomics data within transformer-based molecular generative frameworks, providing a concrete demonstration that ethnopharmacologically informed auxiliary datasets can expand chemical space exploration without compromising predicted target affinity—advancing beyond prior efforts limited to naïve dataset merging or lacking robust cross-domain evaluation.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA: Integrating multi-modal biological data embeddings (genomics and transcriptomics) into conditional Transformer-based generative models will yield novel drug candidates with significantly improved in vitro efficacy compared to models trained solely on molecular structures.

BACKGROUND: Current generative AI approaches for drug discovery primarily rely on molecular structure data, limiting their ability to capture complex biological contexts that influence drug efficacy. Incorporating rich biological profiles such as genomics and transcriptomics alongside chemical structures could enable more precise design of molecules tailored to specific disease-relevant cellular environments. However, systematic evidence that such multi-modal integration improves experimentally validated drug efficacy remains lacking.

APPROACH: We develop a conditional Transformer molecular generator that fuses learned embeddings from genomics and transcriptomics data (derived via variational autoencoders) with molecular structure embeddings through cross-attention layers, enabling generation of candidate molecules conditioned on biological context. A baseline Transformer model trained only on molecular structures serves as comparator. Both models undergo in silico triaging with advanced ADMET and synthetic accessibility filters before candidate synthesis.

EXPERIMENT: Using the Open Cancer Therapeutics Consortium (OCTC) dataset for training and the GDSC dataset for validation, we generate and synthesize top 100 candidates per model targeting selected cancer cell lines. We then measure in vitro IC50 values via viability assays, comparing median IC50 distributions using Wilcoxon rank-sum tests (p < 0.01) to assess efficacy improvements. Additional metrics include molecular diversity (Tanimoto similarity) and synthesizability scores.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior generative AI drug design studies that focus on molecular structures alone or lack rigorous experimental validation, this work explicitly integrates multi-omics embeddings with chemical structure in a novel cross-attention Transformer framework, employs comprehensive compound prioritization controls, and establishes falsifiability through statistically powered in vitro bioassays. This approach directly addresses biological context influence on drug response, advancing multi-modal generative modeling toward practical drug discovery impact.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA: Integrating generative AI with scalable quantum chemistry-informed surrogate models to iteratively optimize drug-like molecules for improved binding affinity, validated by comprehensive in vitro assays including binding and drug-like property profiling.

BACKGROUND: Current generative AI approaches for drug discovery primarily optimize molecules using docking scores, which often lack the accuracy needed to predict true binding affinities. While quantum chemistry (QC) methods offer higher fidelity binding energy estimations, their high computational cost limits throughput and practical integration into iterative generative pipelines, leaving a gap in effectively combining AI-driven design with accurate molecular interaction modeling.

APPROACH: We propose a novel hybrid framework where a transformer-based generative model fine-tuned on ChEMBL kinase inhibitors is iteratively guided by a machine-learned surrogate model trained on QC (DFT-level QM/MM) calculated binding energies, enabling rapid approximation of QC feedback without incurring prohibitive computational expense. This surrogate-driven feedback loop biases molecule generation toward candidates with improved QC-predicted affinities, which are then validated through detailed DFT QM/MM calculations before experimental synthesis and testing.

EXPERIMENT: Using the ChEMBL 29 kinase inhibitor dataset and PDBBind v2020 refined set for training and evaluation, we will compare molecules generated by (1) the QC surrogate-integrated generative pipeline versus (2) a baseline generative model optimized only on docking scores (AutoDock Vina), assessing binding affinities via SPR assays alongside solubility and stability measurements. Statistical significance of affinity improvements (Kd reductions) will be evaluated with paired tests, incorporating negative and random controls, and blinded experimental protocols to ensure robustness.

NOVELTY: This work goes beyond prior efforts by introducing a scalable QC surrogate model to enable iterative QC-guided molecule generation, overcoming the computational bottleneck of direct DFT QM/MM integration. It rigorously combines theoretical QC gradients with generative reinforcement learning in a closed loop, validated by a comprehensive experimental pipeline that extends beyond binding affinity to key drug-like properties, thus bridging the gap between in silico design and practical drug discovery outcomes.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Investigate how the proportion of domain-specific continual pre-training data influences Large Language Model performance by characterizing diminishing returns through domain-adaptive scaling laws grounded in distributional shifts and effective dataset quality, rather than naive power-law fits on perplexity.

BACKGROUND:  
While continual pre-training (CPT) with domain-specific data improves LLM capabilities on specialized tasks, it remains unclear how increasing the domain data fraction affects overall scaling behavior and when benefits saturate. Existing work often treats domain data fraction as a scalar scaling variable without accounting for domain distribution shifts or controlling for dataset size and quality, leading to ambiguous interpretations of scaling exponents. Clarifying the relationship between domain data ratio and model gains is crucial for efficient CPT data curation and compute allocation.

APPROACH:  
We propose a rigorous experimental framework that treats domain-specific data ratio as a factor inducing distributional shift rather than a direct scaling variable. By mixing general (Pile) and domain (CodeSearchNet) corpora at controlled ratios, we compare performance gains against size-matched random subsets of general data to isolate domain specificity effects. We analyze model perplexity on held-out domain validation sets alongside explicit domain divergence metrics, using piecewise regression and statistical tests to identify diminishing returns thresholds in effective data quality rather than raw data fraction.

EXPERIMENT:  
Fine-tune GPT-2 Large pretrained on the Pile with CPT mixtures containing 0%, 10%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 90%, and 100% CodeSearchNet data, alongside matched-size random subsets of Pile-only data. Evaluate on CodeSearchNet validation perplexity and measure domain divergence via KL divergence on token distributions. Use multiple seeds and fixed training steps with consistent hyperparameters, applying piecewise linear models to detect inflection points in performance improvements attributable to domain data ratio.

NOVELTY:  
This work grounds domain-specific CPT scaling analysis in theoretical perspectives on distributional shifts and dataset quality, introducing rigorous baselines and statistical validation to move beyond heuristic power-law fits. It explicitly quantifies a “critical threshold” for domain data utility, offering practical guidance for balanced CPT data mixtures and extending prior scaling laws by integrating domain adaptation theory and effective dataset complexity.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Investigate how domain-specific continual pre-training (CPT) data volume and domain diversity impact scaling behavior of large language models, demonstrating that diminishing returns on limited-coverage benchmarks can mask persistent or differing gains revealed by diverse, high-quality domain data and more sensitive evaluation metrics.

BACKGROUND:  
While scaling laws for LLMs characterize performance improvements with increasing model size, data, and compute, it remains unclear how these laws apply to domain-specific continual pre-training, especially when evaluation benchmarks vary in domain coverage and sensitivity. Prior work shows CPT can improve domain tasks, but often with limited analysis of how data volume thresholds and domain diversity interact or how benchmark choice affects perceived returns. Understanding these interactions is crucial to optimize CPT strategies under practical compute constraints.

APPROACH:  
We propose to conduct controlled CPT experiments on a fixed base biomedical LLM (PubMedBERT) varying CPT data volume (0.5B, 1B, 2B tokens) and domain composition (PubMed Central alone vs. combined PubMed Central, MIMIC-III, and PubMed abstracts). Model performance will be evaluated zero-shot and with task-specific fine-tuning on benchmarks spanning limited to broad domain coverage and complexity (BLURB, MedNLI, BioASQ), using sensitive metrics (F1, accuracy, MRR) and statistical significance testing to rigorously assess diminishing returns and domain diversity effects.

EXPERIMENT:  
CPT is performed separately on single-domain and mixed-domain corpora, controlling for training hyperparameters and data quality; evaluation includes zero-shot and fine-tuned probing on BLURB (limited coverage), MedNLI (natural language inference), and BioASQ (diverse biomedical QA). Diminishing returns are defined quantitatively as <1% absolute gain upon doubling data volume on BLURB, contrasted with sustained gains on broader benchmarks. Ablations with shuffled data subsets and domain overlap quantification isolate data volume versus diversity contributions.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely integrates domain diversity and evaluation sensitivity into the empirical scaling law framework for CPT, clarifying how domain-specific data volume thresholds depend on benchmark characteristics. Unlike prior scaling studies focusing on aggregate or general-domain data, we dissect nuanced CPT scaling behaviors in biomedical NLP, providing practical guidance for efficient domain adaptation and advancing theoretical understanding of LLM domain specialization.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Investigate how optimal compute allocation between general and domain-specific data for scaling large language models depends on domain specificity, modulated by rigorously controlled dataset quality and domain complexity, by training matched-compute transformer LLMs on systematically varied data mixtures across heterogeneous domains.

BACKGROUND:  
While scaling laws for large language models (LLMs) establish how model size, dataset size, and compute jointly determine performance, the role of domain specificity in optimal compute allocation remains unclear, especially when considering dataset quality and domain complexity. Existing work, such as D-CPT, focuses on heuristic or grid-searched mixture ratios for continual pretraining without principled control or theoretical grounding, leaving open whether domain-specific models require distinct compute-data trade-offs compared to general models. A rigorous, cross-domain empirical and theoretical framework is needed to clarify if and how domain characteristics modulate optimal scaling strategies.

APPROACH:  
We propose to train transformer-based LLMs (1B and 3B parameters) from scratch under fixed compute budgets on corpora with varied mixtures of general (Pile) and domain-specific data from three domains with differing complexity: biomedical (PubMed Central Open Access), mathematical (MATH + arXiv math papers), and general (Pile). Dataset quality and domain complexity will be quantified and controlled using multi-dimensional, domain-agnostic metrics including entropy, perplexity, syntactic diversity, semantic density, and intrinsic dimensionality, validated externally to ensure comparability. We will also implement curriculum learning schedules that progressively adjust domain data proportion. Performance will be evaluated on zero-shot and few-shot domain benchmarks—BioASQ factoid QA for biomedical, MATH problem-solving accuracy for mathematics, and LAMBADA for general language modeling.

EXPERIMENT:  
For each domain, we train models from scratch on mixture ratios of domain to general data (0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 100%) with matched compute, comparing baselines trained exclusively on general or domain corpora and D-CPT-like grid search models. Statistical significance (p < 0.01) in downstream zero- and few-shot metrics will determine success. Falsification requires that optimal mixture ratios do not differ significantly across domains or correlate with domain complexity or dataset quality, indicating domain specificity does not modulate optimal compute allocation.

NOVELTY:  
This study goes beyond prior heuristic and single-domain approaches by rigorously quantifying and controlling dataset quality and domain complexity across multiple domains, situating the hypothesis explicitly within scaling law theory, and jointly modeling compute, data composition, and domain characteristics. This provides the first principled, cross-domain empirical and theoretical investigation into how domain specificity fundamentally shapes compute-data trade-offs in LLM scaling.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Incorporating domain-specific knowledge graphs into large language model pretraining yields complementary, task- and fusion-strategy-dependent benefits that vary smoothly with model scale, without exhibiting a universal nonlinear inversion in gains beyond a fixed parameter threshold.

BACKGROUND:  
While scaling laws have characterized performance improvements of large language models with model size and data, it remains unclear how structured domain knowledge from knowledge graphs (KGs) interacts with these scaling trends. Prior KG-LLM integration methods demonstrate task-dependent gains but lack a principled understanding of how fusion strategies and model scale jointly affect performance across domains. This gap complicates efforts to predict when and how KG integration is most effective, limiting theoretical and practical guidance for domain-specific LLM development.

APPROACH:  
We propose a staged, resource-aware experimental framework that pretrains decoder-only LLMs at three scales (125M, 1B, 10B parameters) on biomedical and software engineering corpora, integrating their respective KGs (UMLS and CodeOntology) via three distinct fusion strategies—early, mid-level (KG-aware attention), and late fusion (output head grounding). Controlled ablations with randomized KG embeddings and no-KG baselines isolate the impact of KG incorporation. Models are fine-tuned and evaluated on well-established downstream tasks including BioASQ (QA), BC5CDR (NER), ChemProt (relation extraction), and CodeSearchNet tasks (code search and summarization).

EXPERIMENT:  
Performance is systematically measured using task-specific metrics—F1 for NER and relation extraction, accuracy and mean reciprocal rank for code search, and ROUGE for summarization—across model sizes and fusion methods. Hypothesis falsification occurs if gains from KG integration exhibit a sharp nonlinear inversion at a fixed parameter count across all tasks and fusion techniques, or if fusion strategy and task have negligible effect relative to model size alone.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that either focuses narrowly on single fusion methods or domains or treats scaling laws without KG integration, this study uniquely combines multiple fusion strategies with rigorous controls across model scales and domains to elucidate nuanced, multifactorial interactions between KG integration, model size, and downstream task performance—offering the first principled framework to comprehensively characterize knowledge graph benefits in LLM scaling behavior.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
The optimal scaling law for Large Language Models’ domain adaptation performance exhibits a nonlinear regime with a sharp inflection once the domain corpus proportion surpasses 30% of training data, fundamentally deviating from the smooth power-law scaling observed in mixed-domain training.

BACKGROUND:  
While prior work (Kaplan et al., 2020; D-CPT Law, 2024) establishes that LLM performance scales as a power law with data and model size across mixed-domain corpora, it remains unclear whether this scaling continues uniformly when domain-specific data dominates training. Determining if and when domain mixture ratios induce qualitative shifts in scaling behavior is critical for optimizing domain-adaptive pretraining strategies without exhaustive grid searches or oversimplified assumptions about mixture linearity.

APPROACH:  
We systematically vary the proportion of domain-specific data (MATH for mathematical reasoning, CodeSearchNet for code generation) within a mixed training corpus (Pile+domain) from 0% to 60% in 10% increments, training multiple LLM sizes (125M, 355M, 1.3B parameters) via cost-efficient fine-tuning from a shared general-domain checkpoint. Performance is evaluated on domain-specific perplexity and zero-shot accuracy benchmarks (MATH, HumanEval), fitting nonlinear regression models to detect statistically significant deviations (>5% relative gap, p<0.05) from baseline power-law predictions.

EXPERIMENT:  
Models will be fine-tuned under controlled compute budgets with fixed hyperparameters from the “Predictable Scale” step-law framework. Domain ratios are precisely managed using reproducible data sampling pipelines to avoid overlap or leakage. Statistical power analysis and multiple hypothesis corrections ensure robust detection of nonlinear scaling regimes in domain adaptation performance curves.

NOVELTY:  
This research uniquely combines principled theoretical motivation linking domain corpus dominance to nonlinear representational learning dynamics with a rigorously controlled, resource-efficient experimental design—advancing beyond prior work’s reliance on grid search or uniform mixture assumptions to uncover fundamental regime shifts in LLM scaling behavior for domain adaptation.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Foundation models pre-trained on integrated multimodal single-cell data (transcriptomics, epigenomics, and proteomics) will significantly outperform unimodal models in predicting cell-type-specific gene regulatory networks, as validated by large-scale, matched CRISPR perturbation datasets.

BACKGROUND:  
Accurate inference of cell-type-specific gene regulatory networks (GRNs) remains a critical open challenge in single-cell biology due to complex regulatory mechanisms that span multiple molecular layers. While unimodal approaches (e.g., scRNA-seq or scATAC-seq alone) provide partial insights, they often lack the complementary information necessary to resolve regulatory interactions with high precision. Multimodal single-cell datasets now enable integrated analyses, but it remains unclear whether foundation models trained on such data substantially improve GRN inference validated by functional perturbation experiments.

APPROACH:  
We will develop a transformer-based foundation model architecture with cross-modal attention layers to jointly embed matched scRNA-seq, scATAC-seq, and CITE-seq proteomic profiles from human bone marrow cells (Human Cell Atlas). This multimodal model will be fine-tuned to predict gene regulatory edges using attention- and graph-based decoding layers. We will compare performance against unimodal baselines trained independently on scRNA-seq (using GENIE3, GRNBoost2) or scATAC-seq data (using Cicero) with matched model capacity and training regimes to isolate the benefit of multimodal integration.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluation will leverage large-scale Perturb-seq datasets with matched multimodal readouts (e.g., Datlinger et al., 2017; Mimitou et al., 2021) as ground truth for regulatory effects of CRISPR perturbations in defined cell types. Models will be benchmarked using precision-recall (AUPRC) and ROC (AUROC) curves for predicting validated regulatory edges. Success is defined as a ≥10% relative increase in AUPRC over the best unimodal model; failure to exceed a 5% improvement or achieve statistical significance (p > 0.05 by bootstrap) will falsify the hypothesis. Additional controls include modality-shuffled multimodal models and architecture-matched unimodal versions.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely integrates multimodal single-cell data into a foundation model framework with cross-modal attention tailored for GRN inference and rigorously validates predictions using matched, multimodal CRISPR perturbation data—a stringent ground truth rarely leveraged in prior multimodal GRN studies. Furthermore, it systematically controls for model capacity and modality contribution, addressing limitations of previous approaches that lacked either multimodal validation or robust comparison baselines.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Foundation models pre-trained on rigorously integrated multi-omics single-cell datasets—including scRNA-seq, scATAC-seq, and single-cell methylation—achieve significantly better prediction of cell-type-specific regulatory elements than models trained solely on transcriptomic data.

BACKGROUND:  
Predicting regulatory element activity at single-cell resolution is critical for understanding gene regulation in heterogeneous tissues, yet existing models predominantly rely on transcriptomic data alone, which incompletely capture epigenetic regulatory mechanisms. Integrating multiple modalities reflecting chromatin accessibility and DNA methylation promises richer representations of cell states but presents challenges in data sparsity, noise, and alignment, limiting their effective use in foundation models for genomics.

APPROACH:  
We propose a transformer-based foundation model architecture with modality-specific embeddings and modality-aware cross-attention layers that jointly embed scRNA-seq, scATAC-seq, and methylation data after applying advanced multi-modal alignment and imputation methods (e.g., joint variational autoencoders with cell-level matching) to harmonize datasets like the Human Cell Atlas multi-omics and 10x Genomics Multiome. Masked token prediction objectives are modality-tailored (genes, peaks, CpG sites) during pre-training to learn inter-modality regulatory interactions, followed by fine-tuning on supervised cell-type-specific regulatory element prediction tasks defined by high-confidence ATAC-seq peak activity and motif enrichment.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will compare multi-omics foundation models to transcriptomics-only models with identical architectures, as well as classical baselines (random forests, gradient boosting) and state-of-the-art methods (scBasset, scAI), evaluating AUPRC for regulatory element prediction across held-out tissues and cell types in HCA and 10x Multiome datasets. Ablation studies removing methylation or accessibility data will isolate modality contributions. Statistical significance will be assessed via paired bootstrap tests, with a ≥5 percentage point AUPRC improvement over transcriptomics-only models required to support the hypothesis.

NOVELTY:  
This work advances prior efforts by explicitly addressing multi-omics single-cell data integration challenges through robust alignment and joint embedding strategies within a novel transformer framework optimized for sparse, modality-diverse inputs. It uniquely demonstrates the non-redundant predictive value of integrating methylation and chromatin accessibility with transcriptomics for regulatory element inference, surpassing existing single-modality and sequence-only models in downstream tasks.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a multi-modal foundation model jointly trained on genomic sequences and matched cell-type-specific single-cell transcriptomic embeddings to improve prediction of enhancer activity in a cell-type-resolved manner beyond sequence-only or transcriptome-only models.

BACKGROUND:  
Accurately identifying cell-type-specific regulatory elements such as enhancers remains a critical challenge in genomics and single-cell biology, as most existing models rely either on nucleotide sequence or on transcriptomic data alone. Integrating genomic context with cell-type-resolved expression profiles promises to capture complex regulatory logic underlying enhancer function, but current multi-modal models often lack rigorous validation on experimentally measured enhancer activity across diverse primary human cell types. This work addresses the need for robust, interpretable predictive frameworks leveraging matched multi-omic datasets.

APPROACH:  
We propose a cross-attention transformer-based foundation model that encodes enhancer candidate sequences and integrates them with single-cell RNA-seq-derived cell-type embeddings, linked via chromatin accessibility profiles from single-cell ATAC-seq, to jointly learn regulatory patterns predictive of enhancer activity. The model will be trained end-to-end to predict quantitative enhancer activity scores from massively parallel reporter assay (MPRA) datasets specific to human primary cells, using rigorous feature selection and normalization to optimize multi-modal integration.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will compare three models trained on: (1) enhancer sequences alone (e.g., Enformer-like transformer), (2) cell-type-level transcriptomic embeddings alone (e.g., scBERT-based), and (3) the multi-modal joint transformer with cross-attention layers using paired sequence and transcriptomic data drawn from ENCODE MPRA datasets, Human Cell Atlas Tabula Sapiens single-cell RNA-seq, and 10x Genomics PBMC Multiome ATAC+RNA data. Performance will be assessed on held-out chromosomes and cell types using Pearson correlation between predicted and experimentally measured enhancer activities, with statistical significance tested via paired permutation tests (p < 0.01) and supplemented by orthogonal validations from CRISPR perturbation screens where available.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work, our approach uniquely integrates nucleotide-level sequence transformers with cell-type-specific single-cell transcriptomic embeddings through cross-attention layers, enabling the model to capture combinatorial regulatory logic inaccessible to unimodal or simpler concatenation-based models. We explicitly leverage rigorously matched multi-omic datasets and experimentally validated enhancer activities to provide the most comprehensive and biologically grounded foundation model for cell-type-specific regulatory prediction to date.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Foundation models pretrained on integrated multi-omics single-cell datasets (transcriptomic and chromatin accessibility) capture cellular state transitions and lineage trajectories more accurately than models trained solely on transcriptomic data, as demonstrated through improved prediction of experimentally validated pseudotime orderings and branching events.

BACKGROUND:  
Accurately modeling cellular differentiation trajectories from single-cell data is critical for understanding development and disease, yet existing methods often rely on transcriptomic profiles alone, neglecting complementary epigenetic information. While multi-omics single-cell profiling is increasingly available, it remains unclear whether foundation models integrating multiple modalities can better resolve lineage dynamics, especially given modality-specific noise and temporal differences. This work addresses the open problem of leveraging multi-omics integration within deep learning frameworks to robustly infer cellular state transitions validated by biological ground truth.

APPROACH:  
We propose a multi-encoder transformer-based foundation model that separately embeds RNA-seq and ATAC-seq features through modality-specific transformer blocks, integrates them via cross-attention layers into a unified latent representation, and is pretrained using masked token prediction alongside contrastive learning to align modalities per cell. A matched unimodal foundation model will be pretrained only on RNA modalities for direct comparison. Both models will be fine-tuned on lineage trajectory prediction tasks using regression heads for pseudotime and graph neural network layers to capture branching structure, explicitly modeling differentiation dynamics from multi-omics data.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will benchmark on publicly available, experimentally validated multi-omics single-cell datasets with known lineage trajectories, including the SHARE-seq mouse skin differentiation dataset and the 10x Genomics human PBMC Multiome dataset. Evaluation metrics include Spearman’s rank correlation (>0.8 threshold) for pseudotime prediction, Adjusted Rand Index and F1 scores for branching point detection against validated lineage trees, and trajectory topology similarity metrics (e.g., geodesic distance correlation, graph edit distance). Controls include unimodal ATAC-only models, randomized multi-omics pairings, and classical trajectory inference methods (Monocle 3, Slingshot) as well as state-of-the-art unimodal deep models (scVI, scANVI). Statistical significance will be assessed via paired bootstrap tests (p < 0.05). Ablation studies will dissect the contribution of integration strategies (concatenation vs. cross-attention) and modality inclusion.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely combines modality-specific transformers with cross-attention within a foundation model framework pretrained jointly on multi-omics single-cell data, applying rigorous orthogonal validation and uncertainty quantification to lineage trajectories. Unlike prior models focused on unimodal data or shallow integration, it provides a mechanistically motivated architecture and extensive ablations to isolate the impact of epigenetic information on trajectory inference. It also systematically benchmarks against classical and deep learning baselines using topology-aware metrics, thereby advancing interpretability and practical utility in single-cell multi-omics analysis.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Foundation models pretrained on multimodal single-cell data (joint transcriptomics and epigenomics) provide significantly more accurate and biologically valid cell-type-specific gene regulatory network (GRN) predictions than unimodal models trained on single modalities alone.

BACKGROUND:  
Accurately inferring cell-type-specific GRNs remains a critical challenge in single-cell biology, with most existing methods relying on unimodal data such as scRNA-seq or scATAC-seq separately. Multimodal single-cell technologies enable joint measurements of transcriptomic and epigenomic states, suggesting that integrated models could capture regulatory relationships more comprehensively. However, it remains unclear whether foundation models pretrained on such multimodal data can outperform unimodal approaches in predictive accuracy and biological interpretability of GRNs.

APPROACH:  
We will develop a transformer-based foundation model pretrained on paired scRNA-seq and scATAC-seq data via masked token prediction and cross-modal contrastive learning to jointly embed multimodal features per cell. GRN inference will leverage integrated attention and feature attributions with rigorous pruning strategies to extract confident regulatory interactions. Identical architecture unimodal models pretrained separately on only scRNA-seq or scATAC-seq data will serve as direct baselines, alongside classical methods SCENIC (scRNA-seq) and Cicero (scATAC-seq) as non-foundation baselines.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will benchmark models on the 10x Genomics Multiome PBMC dataset and the SHARE-seq skin dataset, evaluating predicted TF-target regulatory edges against curated, cell-type-specific GRN references from ENCODE, TRRUST, and orthogonal perturbation datasets. Metrics include area under the precision-recall curve (AUPR) and area under the ROC curve (AUROC) computed per cell type (e.g., B cells, T cells, monocytes). Success requires the multimodal foundation model to achieve at least a 10% relative improvement in AUPR over the best unimodal baseline with statistical significance (p < 0.05 via bootstrap resampling). Ablation studies (e.g., removing contrastive learning, shuffled modalities, unimodal feature concatenation) and negative controls will isolate the impact of multimodal integration.

NOVELTY:  
This work explicitly establishes a rigorous theoretical and empirical framework demonstrating that foundation models pretrained on multimodal single-cell data yield superior GRN inference, beyond prior unimodal or heuristic multimodal integration methods. Our approach uniquely combines cross-modal contrastive pretraining with interpretable attention-based GRN extraction validated against high-confidence, cell-type-specific ground truths and orthogonal perturbation benchmarks.

topic: Mechanistic interpretability of transformer models
text:
IDEA:  
Systematically identify and prune modular subgraphs within fine-tuned GPT-2 small models that correspond to discrete algorithmic primitives, validating mechanistic interpretability by demonstrating task performance retention significantly beyond standard pruning baselines.

BACKGROUND:  
Mechanistic interpretability aims to reverse-engineer neural networks into understandable algorithmic components, but it remains unclear whether transformer functional circuits are modular and correspond to discrete algorithmic primitives. Existing efforts either rely on manual circuit discovery or provide limited validation of modularity, leaving open whether pruning connectivity in identified subgraphs can reveal modular, functionally sufficient units without degrading task performance. Addressing this gap is critical to rigorously linking internal circuits to interpretable algorithms rather than noisy, distributed representations.

APPROACH:  
We will represent GPT-2 small as a directed neuron-connection graph and leverage an ensemble of interpretability techniques—including integrated gradients, activation patching, and functional ablations—to robustly identify candidate subgraphs associated with fundamental algorithmic operations (e.g., "reduce" and "map" in ListOps). Targeted structured pruning will be applied to these subgraphs, followed by standardized low-rate fine-tuning to recover performance. Comparisons to rigorously matched random and magnitude-based pruning controls, in terms of task accuracy and fine-grained functional assays, will test the modular sufficiency of the candidate subgraphs.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will fine-tune GPT-2 small (124M parameters) on the ListOps dataset and the SCONE dataset, both requiring discrete algorithmic reasoning. Success is defined as maintaining ≥95% of baseline accuracy after pruning ~30-40% of connections in identified subgraphs, outperforming random and magnitude pruning controls matched on size and structure. Ablation studies and intermediate activation analyses will further assess the distinctness and functional boundaries of these subgraphs.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work focused on manual circuit discovery or generic pruning, this study rigorously combines multiple interpretability tools to isolate and validate modular subgraphs as discrete algorithmic primitives via pruning-based functional sufficiency tests. This approach explicitly links pruning performance retention to mechanistic interpretability, distinguishing modular algorithmic units from general network redundancy and advancing automated, reproducible circuit discovery in transformers.

topic: Mechanistic interpretability of transformer models
text:
IDEA:  
Transformer models trained on multilingual corpora develop shared mechanistic circuits in early attention layers that encode language-agnostic syntactic or semantic computations, which can be falsified if layerwise circuit activations diverge significantly across typologically distinct languages.

BACKGROUND:  
Understanding whether multilingual transformers internally represent linguistic information through shared mechanistic circuits across languages is an open problem in mechanistic interpretability and cross-lingual representation learning. While prior work has identified meaningful circuits within monolingual or English-trained models, it remains unclear if these circuits generalize mechanistically across languages rather than reflecting superficial correlations. Establishing or refuting this would advance interpretability and our understanding of multilingual model generalization.

APPROACH:  
We rigorously identify mechanistic circuits in early attention layers (layers 1–3) of a pretrained XLM-R base model using activation patching and causal interventions on English inputs from the Flores-101 parallel corpus, focusing on neuron groups and attention heads implicated in syntactic probing tasks. We then extract circuit activation vectors on aligned parallel sentences in typologically diverse languages, controlling for tokenization, sentence length, and syntactic complexity, and compute cosine similarity scores between language pairs. Complementary causal ablations and cross-lingual connectivity analyses verify circuit equivalence beyond activation similarity.

EXPERIMENT:  
Using Flores-101 parallel corpora for English, Arabic, Chinese, Finnish, and Swahili, we measure circuit activation similarity scores in XLM-R (multilingual), comparing against monolingual RoBERTa fine-tuned on English and an untrained XLM-R baseline. We establish statistically calibrated thresholds based on within-language activation variability and shuffled-language controls, interpreting similarity >0.75 as evidence of shared circuits and <0.5 as falsification of the hypothesis.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely combines causal mechanistic interpretability techniques with rigorous cross-lingual alignment and baseline calibration to test mechanistic circuit generalization in multilingual transformers, extending beyond previous descriptive or monolingual analyses and grounding claims in robust causal and statistical validation across typologically diverse languages.

topic: Mechanistic interpretability of transformer models
text:
IDEA:  
Transformer attention heads that specialize in syntactic feature detection constitute stable, mechanistically meaningful subnetworks that are reproducibly identifiable across diverse transformer architectures and training corpora, as demonstrated by statistically validated probe-driven selection and cross-model aligned activation similarity analysis.

BACKGROUND:  
Understanding whether syntactic processing in transformers is localized to stable, reusable subnetworks across models remains an open challenge in mechanistic interpretability. Prior work has identified syntactic-specialist heads within individual models but lacks rigorous, architecture-aware criteria for defining these heads and principled methods for cross-model comparison. Clarifying whether syntactic mechanisms generalize across architectures and datasets is critical for advancing interpretable NLP models and mechanistic explanations.

APPROACH:  
We first define syntactic-specialist heads via statistically calibrated probing methods that incorporate permutation testing and probe ablations on the Universal Dependencies English Web Treebank, ensuring robustness across GPT-2 Medium, RoBERTa Base, and T5 Base. Next, we employ linear representation alignment techniques such as Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA) and Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) to compare activations of these heads on held-out Penn Treebank data, quantifying functional equivalence beyond naïve cosine similarity. Controls include non-syntactic specialist heads, random heads, and untrained models to contextualize similarities.

EXPERIMENT:  
We test the hypothesis by measuring (1) the proportion of syntactic-specialist heads in one model that have a significantly aligned counterpart (e.g., CCA similarity above empirically derived thresholds) in another model, and (2) whether these syntactic head pairs exhibit higher alignment than baselines (random, non-syntactic heads) using permutation tests (p < 0.01). Stability is further validated by within-model cross-dataset probing and comparing trained versus untrained models, using the Universal Dependencies and Penn Treebank datasets.

NOVELTY:  
This work introduces statistically rigorous, architecture-aware criteria for identifying mechanistically meaningful syntactic heads and applies advanced cross-model representation alignment methods to test functional reuse across heterogeneous transformer models and corpora. It advances beyond prior mechanistic interpretability studies by providing principled falsifiability, stronger controls, and a clearer theoretical connection between syntactic specialization and reusable subnetworks.

topic: Mechanistic interpretability of transformer models
text:
IDEA:  
We propose to rigorously test whether transformer attention heads responsible for syntactic parsing encode discrete, fully extractable symbolic grammar rules that can reconstruct parse trees with near-lossless fidelity compared to the original model’s predictions.

BACKGROUND:  
While transformer language models exhibit strong syntactic parsing capabilities, it remains an open question whether this knowledge is stored as discrete, algorithmic rules or distributed, continuous representations. Prior work on structural probes and mechanistic interpretability has revealed correlations between attention patterns and syntactic structures, but none have demonstrated a complete, lossless symbolic extraction of parsing behavior solely from attention heads. Clarifying this would illuminate the nature of syntactic representations in transformers and advance fundamental understanding of neural language models.

APPROACH:  
We will identify attention heads strongly correlated with syntactic parsing in a RoBERTa-base model fine-tuned on the Penn Treebank (constituent parsing) and Universal Dependencies English-EWT (dependency parsing) datasets via attention probing, activation patching, and causal mediation analysis. These heads’ attention patterns will be algorithmically transformed into discrete symbolic grammar rules or transition-based parser actions (e.g., CFG rules or shift-reduce operations), enabling reconstruction of parse trees without recourse to the original neural weights or activations.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate parsing accuracy by comparing (1) the original transformer’s parse outputs, (2) parse trees generated purely from the extracted symbolic rules, and (3) strong baseline parsers such as the Berkeley Neural Parser, using labeled F1 for constituent parsing and labeled attachment score (LAS) for dependency parsing on held-out PTB and UD-EWT test sets. Rigorous controls including random attention baselines, ablations of identified heads, and statistical significance testing will validate the extraction pipeline and its generalization.

NOVELTY:  
This work advances beyond prior structural probes and partial mechanistic studies by aiming for a complete, algorithmic symbolic extraction of transformer parsing behavior from attention heads alone, explicitly testing whether syntactic knowledge is representable as discrete symbolic grammar without loss of fidelity—an ambitious and previously unproven hypothesis in mechanistic interpretability.

topic: Mechanistic interpretability of transformer models
text:
IDEA:  
Functional circuits in transformer language models predominantly localize to identifiable subnetworks corresponding to discrete syntactic and semantic tasks rather than being diffusely distributed across all parameters; this can be tested and potentially falsified by targeted causal ablations combined with robust subnetwork identification and comprehensive controls.

BACKGROUND:  
Understanding whether transformer model functionality is modular—localized in subnetworks dedicated to particular linguistic phenomena—or fully distributed remains a core open question in mechanistic interpretability. Despite prior isolated circuit discoveries, the field lacks a systematic, validated pipeline to isolate stable subnetworks whose disruption selectively impairs specific syntactic or semantic capabilities. Resolving this will clarify the internal organization of language models and inform interpretability and model editing efforts.

APPROACH:  
We develop and rigorously validate a reproducible subnetwork identification pipeline combining activation patching, causal mediation analysis, and clustering of attention heads and MLP units linked to well-defined linguistic phenomena. Targeted ablations of these subnetworks, alongside matched-size random and sham ablations, quantify selective functional impairment. The pipeline is augmented with stability analyses, plasticity assessments through fine-tuning post-ablation, and fine-grained behavioral error pattern analyses to robustly measure localization.

EXPERIMENT:  
Using a fine-tuned GPT-2 small model, we evaluate subnetwork localization on three linguistically rich datasets—SyntaxGym (syntax), GLUE diagnostic set (semantics), and LAMBADA (discourse-level coherence)—measuring task-specific accuracy and perplexity changes after ablating candidate subnetworks versus controls. Selectivity (>30% degradation on target with <10% off-target) and statistical significance (with multiple hypothesis correction) determine support or falsification of localization.

NOVELTY:  
This work advances beyond prior mechanistic interpretability studies by establishing a validated, statistically rigorous, and reproducible framework for subnetwork discovery and causal testing, integrating comprehensive ablation controls and plasticity evaluations. Unlike prior efforts that focus on isolated circuits or rely on manual intuition, our automated, benchmarked pipeline systematically tests the modularity hypothesis across diverse linguistic phenomena with robust causal evidence.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA: Incorporating lightweight personalized feature transformation layers before local federated model training effectively reduces client-drift and accelerates convergence under heterogeneous non-IID data, outperforming control variate methods like SCAFFOLD.

BACKGROUND: Federated learning on heterogeneous, non-IID client data suffers from client-drift—local models diverge during training due to feature and label distribution skew—resulting in slow convergence and degraded global model performance. While methods like SCAFFOLD reduce drift via control variates that correct local updates, they do not explicitly address input feature heterogeneity, limiting effectiveness in realistic federated scenarios with diverse client data distributions.

APPROACH: We propose that each client learns a small, personalized feature transformation module (e.g., a 1x1 convolution or linear layer with batch normalization) that maps heterogeneous raw inputs into a shared latent space before training a globally federated backbone model. This personalized embedding layer is trained locally and kept private, mitigating distribution mismatch at the feature level and complementing global aggregation. We rigorously analyze how this feature-level adaptation reduces local update variance and client-drift beyond existing personalization and variance reduction techniques.

EXPERIMENT: We will evaluate on LEAF FEMNIST and Google Speech Commands V2 datasets partitioned into 100 clients with Dirichlet non-IID splits (α=0.5). Baselines include FedAvg, SCAFFOLD, and local fine-tuning without feature transformations, all matched for model capacity and tuned hyperparameters. Metrics include final global test accuracy after 200 rounds, convergence speed (rounds to 90% peak accuracy), and direct client-drift measurements (local-global model parameter divergence and gradient variance). Statistical significance (p < 0.05, paired t-test over 5 seeds) with ≥2% accuracy improvement over SCAFFOLD confirms the hypothesis; failure to surpass or match SCAFFOLD within defined margins falsifies it.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work focusing on variance reduction or global-local knowledge distillation, our approach uniquely tackles client-drift by explicitly learning personalized input feature transformations that align heterogeneous client data distributions prior to federated training. This targeted adaptation at the feature level, combined with global model sharing, provides a novel and theoretically substantiated mechanism to reduce client-drift, especially in settings with pronounced feature distribution skew unaddressed by control variate methods like SCAFFOLD.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA: Dynamically adaptive client clustering based on real-time data and model update similarity during federated learning effectively mitigates client drift and significantly improves global model accuracy under heterogeneous non-IID data, outperforming static clustering and advanced FL baselines.

BACKGROUND: Federated learning with heterogeneous, non-IID data suffers from client drift, causing slow, unstable convergence and suboptimal global models. Existing static clustering approaches use fixed client groupings that fail to capture evolving data distributions and client behaviors, limiting their ability to reduce drift and improve accuracy. There is a need for adaptive mechanisms that dynamically re-cluster clients during training to better handle distribution shifts and system heterogeneity.

APPROACH: We propose a federated learning framework where client clustering is dynamically updated every N rounds by computing similarity metrics derived from lightweight, privacy-preserving summaries of clients’ current local model updates and data statistics (e.g., class distribution histograms). The server uses these metrics to perform spectral or hierarchical clustering, enabling clusters to adapt to evolving client distributions. Each cluster aggregates models internally before a weighted global aggregation, reducing client drift more effectively than static clusters or no clustering.

EXPERIMENT: We evaluate on two heterogeneous benchmarks—CIFAR-10 partitioned with a Dirichlet distribution (α=0.1) for strong non-IID image classification, and the LEAF Shakespeare dataset for user-level NLP next-character prediction—comparing our method against FedAvg, static clustering, FedProx, and SCAFFOLD baselines. Metrics include final global model accuracy (targeting >5% and >3% improvements over baselines for CIFAR-10 and Shakespeare, respectively), convergence speed (rounds to 90% peak accuracy), and client drift measured by a rigorously defined and normalized model update divergence metric validated against empirical performance. Communication and computational overheads of dynamic clustering are quantified and analyzed to ensure practical feasibility.

NOVELTY: Unlike previous static or heuristic clustering methods, our approach continuously adapts client clusters based on real-time model and data shifts, explicitly addressing non-stationary heterogeneity in federated learning. We provide a theoretical convergence analysis of dynamic hierarchical clustering’s impact on stability and bias-variance tradeoffs, supported by robust drift metrics and extensive ablations, setting our work apart from prior FL drift mitigation techniques like SCAFFOLD and personalized FL.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA: Incorporating client-specific Batch Normalization layers in federated learning models reduces client-drift and accelerates convergence on heterogeneous non-IID data compared to global Batch Normalization and state-of-the-art drift correction methods like SCAFFOLD.

BACKGROUND: Federated learning with heterogeneous, non-IID client data commonly suffers from client-drift, where local model updates diverge due to differing data distributions, slowing convergence and degrading global model performance. Existing methods like FedAvg with shared global Batch Normalization or variance-reduction techniques (e.g., SCAFFOLD) partially address drift but do not fully adapt feature normalization to client-specific distribution shifts, limiting scalability and robustness.

APPROACH: We propose a federated training framework where Batch Normalization parameters (mean, variance, scale, and shift) are maintained privately per client, while all other model weights are aggregated globally via FedAvg. This isolates normalization statistics from global averaging, enabling client-adaptive feature normalization that better handles covariate and label shifts inherent in heterogeneous data, thereby mitigating client-drift without additional communication overhead.

EXPERIMENT: We evaluate on FEMNIST (naturally heterogeneous handwriting) and CIFAR-10 partitioned by labels to induce severe non-IIDness, simulating 100 clients with varying data sizes and distribution skews. We compare (1) FedAvg with global BN (baseline), (2) FedAvg with client-specific BN (proposed), and (3) SCAFFOLD. Metrics include convergence speed—defined as the average communication rounds to reach 75% of each method’s final test accuracy over 5 independent runs with confidence intervals—and final global test accuracy after 200 rounds. Control experiments will include centralized training with isolated BN layers to disentangle normalization effects from federated dynamics.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work focusing on global normalization or complex drift-correction algorithms, this approach explicitly separates and personalizes normalization parameters per client within a standard FedAvg protocol, providing a theoretically motivated and empirically validated mechanism to reduce drift rooted in feature distribution shifts. This nuanced isolation of BN parameters from global aggregation offers a lightweight, communication-efficient alternative that synergizes with existing optimization corrections, advancing both theoretical understanding and practical performance in heterogeneous federated learning.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA: Incorporate client-specific meta-learning modules that locally adapt model initialization and optimization dynamics to reduce client-drift more effectively than global knowledge distillation in heterogeneous federated learning with non-IID data.

BACKGROUND: Federated learning suffers from client-drift caused by heterogeneous and non-IID data distributions across clients, which slows convergence and degrades global model performance. Existing methods like global knowledge distillation provide static global guidance, but they fail to dynamically tailor training to client-specific data heterogeneity, limiting personalization and convergence speed. There is a need for mechanisms that adaptively personalize local model updates while mitigating client-drift.

APPROACH: We propose augmenting each client with a lightweight meta-learning module inspired by MAML that locally adapts the global model parameters’ initialization and learning rates based on the client’s data distribution during local training, while keeping meta-parameters private. Unlike global knowledge distillation approaches that rely on fixed global teacher signals, our method dynamically personalizes optimization trajectories on each client to reduce drift, with only base model parameters aggregated globally. This maintains personalization and improves adaptation without inter-client meta-parameter communication.

EXPERIMENT: We evaluate on LEAF benchmark datasets FEMNIST and Sentiment140, both exhibiting strong heterogeneity and non-IID distributions, under realistic federated settings with variable client participation and dynamic data shifts. Baselines include FedAvg, SCAFFOLD, and Global Knowledge Distillation (2021). Metrics include final global test accuracy, convergence speed (communication rounds to 95% final accuracy), and personalization quality measured by local client test accuracy. Ablations will compare against fine-tuning personalization and analyze meta-parameter capacity and hyperparameter sensitivity. A significant (>3% absolute) improvement in global accuracy and >20% communication round reduction over GKD will confirm the hypothesis; failure to meet these benchmarks or unstable training will falsify it.

NOVELTY: This work differs from existing personalized FL meta-learning methods (e.g., Per-FedAvg, FedMeta) by explicitly targeting client-drift mitigation through adaptive meta-learned initialization and optimization dynamics that remain fully local, avoiding global meta-aggregation or teacher signals. It rigorously links meta-learning adaptation to theoretical client-drift reduction and rigorously evaluates scalability, computational overhead, and robustness in realistic heterogeneous federated environments, providing a principled alternative to static global distillation approaches.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA: Introducing client-specific adaptive aggregation weights based on cosine similarity to the previous global model update accelerates convergence in federated learning with heterogeneous, non-IID data.

BACKGROUND: Federated learning with non-IID and heterogeneous client data often suffers from client drift, causing slow and unstable convergence of the global model. Existing aggregation schemes like uniform weighting (FedAvg) or static data-size-based weights fail to dynamically account for client update alignment, while algorithms such as SCAFFOLD mitigate drift via control variates but add algorithmic complexity. There is a need for a simple, adaptive aggregation mechanism that effectively exploits inter-client update similarity to reduce convergence time without sacrificing robustness.

APPROACH: We propose dynamically computing aggregation weights each round by measuring the cosine similarity between each client’s model update and the previous global update, shifting the similarity range to non-negative values to form normalized weights. This adaptive scheme prioritizes contributions from clients whose updates align with the global learning trajectory, potentially reducing client drift and improving convergence speed. Initialization is handled by uniform weighting in the first round, and warm-up phases ensure stable early training. Ablations isolate the effect of similarity weighting from confounders like update magnitude and data size.

EXPERIMENT: The method will be evaluated on LEAF’s FEMNIST and CIFAR-10-LT datasets to represent distinct non-IID and imbalanced federated scenarios. Convergence will be measured by the number of communication rounds to reach target global accuracies (75% for FEMNIST, 80% for CIFAR-10-LT), area under the accuracy curve, and final accuracy, averaged over multiple random seeds. Baselines include FedAvg (uniform weights), FedAvg with static data-size proportional weights, and SCAFFOLD. Statistical significance and robustness will be assessed through confidence intervals and ablation studies, with special attention to drift metrics and failed runs.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that uses similarity measures primarily for client selection or knowledge distillation, this approach uniquely leverages the previous global update as a dynamic reference for client-specific aggregation weighting, enabling a lightweight, theoretically motivated drift mitigation without additional control variates or model components. This provides a novel, interpretable mechanism to balance update alignment and useful diversity in heterogeneous federated learning.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA:  
Deep learning emulators trained on multi-scale CESM Large Ensemble outputs, enhanced with domain adaptation, can predict extreme weather event statistics under unseen climate scenarios more accurately than traditional parameterizations and classical machine learning models.

BACKGROUND:  
Current physically-based parameterizations in climate models often struggle to faithfully reproduce extreme weather event statistics, especially under novel climate forcing scenarios. While deep learning emulators have shown promise in capturing subgrid-scale processes, their generalization across different climate models and forcing conditions remains unclear due to dataset shifts and limited evaluation on extremes. Addressing this gap is critical for accelerating climate simulations without sacrificing fidelity in extreme event predictions.

APPROACH:  
We propose a convolutional neural network with residual and temporal convolutional layers trained on CESM Large Ensemble (CESM-LE) outputs to emulate subgrid-scale variables relevant to extremes, such as convective precipitation intensity and near-surface temperature variability. Transfer learning and domain adaptation techniques will be employed to bridge dataset shifts when testing on out-of-sample HighResMIP CMIP6 simulations, improving the emulator’s robustness across differing climate forcings and resolutions. The model will be optimized using combined mean squared error and distribution-focused loss functions to better capture tail behaviors of extremes.

EXPERIMENT:  
The DL emulator will be trained on historical and RCP8.5 CESM-LE data and evaluated first on held-out CESM-LE scenarios using intermediate controls to assess within-model generalization, then tested on out-of-sample HighResMIP CMIP6 simulations. Performance will be quantitatively assessed by reductions in root mean square error (RMSE) of the 99th percentile daily precipitation and annual maximum near-surface temperature statistics across multiple regions, with success defined as at least 20% RMSE improvement over CESM’s Zhang-McFarlane convection scheme and 10% improvement over a random forest baseline. Rigorous extreme value analysis (e.g., Generalized Extreme Value fitting, Peak Over Threshold methods) with bootstrapped confidence intervals and Kolmogorov-Smirnov tests will quantify statistical significance and uncertainty.

NOVELTY:  
This work explicitly addresses the domain and dataset shift challenges between climate models via transfer learning and intermediate validation controls, providing a robust evaluation of DL emulators’ capacity to generalize extreme event statistics across unseen climate forcings. It combines advanced deep learning architectures with rigorous extreme value theory methods for evaluation, going beyond prior studies that focused mostly on within-model emulation or mean state prediction without systematic cross-model validation or tail-focused loss optimization.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA:  
Deep learning emulators trained on harmonized multi-model climate ensembles will generalize more robustly to unseen and extreme climate regimes than those trained on single-model simulations, as evidenced by statistically significant reductions in predictive error across diverse out-of-sample test scenarios.

BACKGROUND:  
Accurate and efficient emulation of subgrid-scale climate processes is critical for accelerating Earth System Model (ESM) simulations, particularly under extreme climate conditions. Existing emulators trained on single-model data often suffer from limited generalization to novel climate regimes due to narrow training distributions and structural model biases. Leveraging multi-model ensembles offers a promising avenue to broaden the training distribution and improve emulator robustness, yet challenges in data harmonization and domain adaptation have limited rigorous evaluation of this hypothesis.

APPROACH:  
We propose to train a convolutional neural network (CNN)-based emulator on a harmonized dataset comprising historical and SSP5-8.5 scenario runs from at least 10 CMIP6 ESMs, capturing a wide spectrum of physical parameterizations and climate sensitivities. A baseline emulator will be trained on an equivalently sized, data-augmented subset from the single-model CESM2. Both emulators will predict subgrid-scale variables such as cloud fraction and convective precipitation from large-scale climate inputs, with careful inter-model normalization and domain adaptation techniques applied to ensure consistent feature distributions.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate emulator generalization on held-out extreme event periods from unseen SSP5-8.5 intervals and an abrupt 4xCO2 forcing scenario from an independent CMIP6 model excluded from training (e.g., UKESM1-0-LL). Performance metrics include root mean squared error (RMSE) and continuous ranked probability score (CRPS) focused on the 95th and 99th percentile extremes of temperature and precipitation anomalies. Statistical significance of at least 10% error reduction by the multi-model emulator over the single-model baseline will be assessed via paired bootstrap hypothesis tests (p < 0.05).

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely integrates rigorous multi-model data harmonization and domain adaptation with deep learning emulation, providing the first systematic falsifiable test of multi-model generalization advantages in extreme climate regimes. By explicitly controlling for training data volume and model capacity, it advances beyond prior studies that focus on single-model or homogeneous training, offering actionable insights for robust climate model acceleration under nonstationary and high-impact conditions.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA: Incorporating physics-informed constraints into spatiotemporal attention-based deep learning emulators, trained on balanced datasets reflecting multiple teleconnection modes, improves the accurate representation and generalization of climate teleconnection patterns compared to standard convolutional and attention-only architectures.

BACKGROUND: Current deep learning emulators for climate models often struggle to capture the complex, large-scale teleconnection patterns critical for accurate climate variability representation. Existing models typically rely on convolutional architectures or attention mechanisms without explicitly embedding physical laws or teleconnection structures, limiting their fidelity and generalization to unseen climate regimes, especially when teleconnection modes are imbalanced in training data.

APPROACH: We develop a spatiotemporal attention-based emulator that integrates physics-informed constraints by embedding differentiable conservation laws (e.g., energy and mass continuity) as soft penalties in the loss function and applying physics-guided attention masks derived from established teleconnection pathways (e.g., ENSO, NAO, PDO). The model is trained on a carefully constructed balanced dataset subsampled from ERA5 reanalysis (1979–2021) to equally represent multiple teleconnection modes, and tested for generalization on CMIP6 historical simulations.

EXPERIMENT: We conduct a controlled comparative evaluation of three models—a standard CNN emulator, an attention-based model without physics-informed constraints, and our physics-informed attention emulator—using ERA5 and CMIP6 datasets. Quantitative metrics include Pearson correlation coefficients (>0.85 threshold) between predicted and observed teleconnection indices, spatial pattern correlation (>0.75 threshold) for teleconnection-related anomaly fields, and RMSE of predicted fields. Ablation studies isolate the effects of physics-informed losses, attention masking, and dataset balancing. The hypothesis is supported if the physics-informed model shows ≥10% median improvement over CNN and ≥5% over attention-only baselines in teleconnection representation and generalization.

NOVELTY: This work uniquely combines explicit physics-based loss augmentation and teleconnection-informed attention masking within a spatiotemporal deep learning framework, leveraging balanced datasets to disentangle data bias from model capacity. Unlike prior studies focusing on subgrid turbulence or generic climate emulation, it systematically integrates climate teleconnection theory into deep learning emulators with rigorous validation and ablation to establish clear, quantitative gains in teleconnection fidelity and generalization.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA:  
Deep learning emulators trained on harmonized multi-model CMIP6 climate simulation ensembles will generalize more accurately to unseen climate regimes—including future scenarios and excluded models—than emulators trained on single-model data, as demonstrated by statistically significant improvements in extreme event frequency prediction metrics.

BACKGROUND:  
Climate model emulators accelerate expensive Earth system simulations but often struggle to generalize across evolving climate regimes and model structural differences, particularly in predicting rare extreme events critical for risk assessment. Existing emulators predominantly rely on single-model training data, limiting their robustness to distribution shifts from future climate change or alternative model physics. Improving emulator generalization across diverse climate conditions and models remains an open challenge for scalable climate projection and decision support.

APPROACH:  
We propose to develop convolutional LSTM deep neural network emulators augmented with attention mechanisms, trained on carefully harmonized, multi-model CMIP6 datasets (CESM2, MPI-ESM1-2-HR, HadGEM3-GC31-LL) covering historical (1850–2014) and future SSP5-8.5 (2015–2100) periods. Extreme events (heatwaves, heavy precipitation) will be defined via a standardized, physically grounded detection protocol to produce consistent target labels. Training will control for data volume and preprocessing across conditions to isolate the impact of multi-model diversity on generalization.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will conduct a controlled comparison between (1) single-model emulators trained individually on each CMIP6 model’s data and (2) a multi-model emulator trained on pooled, harmonized data, evaluating predictive accuracy on held-out future climate scenarios and an entirely unseen CMIP6 model. Success is defined as achieving at least a 10% statistically significant reduction in RMSE and a 5-point statistically significant increase in F1 score for extreme event frequency prediction by the multi-model emulator relative to the best single-model emulator.

NOVELTY:
