This work rigorously integrates multi-model climate simulation ensembles with domain-adapted deep learning architectures and standardized extreme event metrics to explicitly test multi-model training benefits for emulator generalization across temporally and structurally out-of-distribution regimes. It advances beyond prior single-model subgrid-scale emulators by theoretically grounding and empirically validating improved robustness to distribution shifts critical for future climate risk assessment.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA:  
Deep learning emulators trained on carefully harmonized multi-model CMIP6 climate ensemble data, incorporating domain adaptation and bias correction techniques, will demonstrate significantly improved out-of-distribution generalization to unseen future and regional climate regimes compared to emulators trained solely on single-model datasets.

BACKGROUND:  
Current deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration often suffer from limited generalization when applied to novel climate regimes, due in part to training on single-model outputs that embed model-specific biases and lack diverse climate states. Leveraging multi-model ensembles promises richer variability and physical representation but introduces heterogeneity and structural inconsistencies that challenge direct learning. Addressing these issues is critical to develop robust emulators that generalize beyond training distributions, enabling faster and more reliable climate projections under future scenarios.

APPROACH:  
We propose training convolutional neural network emulators with residual connections on multi-model CMIP6 data (CESM2, GISS-E2-1-G, MPI-ESM1-2-HR), augmented by domain adaptation methods such as adversarial feature alignment and model-specific embeddings to reconcile inter-model biases and structural discrepancies. Parallel single-model emulators will be trained on CESM2 data alone with matched training volumes and model capacity. The emulators will predict monthly regional climate variables (surface temperature, precipitation, circulation fields) from large-scale forcings. Precise holdout regimes defined by strict temporal (future SSP5-8.5), spatial (Arctic summers), and scenario splits will be used for rigorous out-of-distribution evaluation.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate predictive skill on the holdout regimes using metrics including root mean squared error (RMSE), mean absolute error (MAE), and coefficient of determination (R²). Statistical significance testing (p < 0.05) will confirm whether the multi-model emulator achieves at least 10% RMSE improvement and higher R² across multiple variables and regions versus the single-model baseline. Baselines include a parametric linear regression emulator and the original CESM2 physics-based subgrid parameterization.

NOVELTY:  
This study is the first to explicitly integrate domain adaptation and bias correction strategies within deep learning emulators trained on multi-model CMIP6 ensembles for climate acceleration, paired with rigorously defined out-of-distribution testing regimes and theoretical framing in transfer learning. Unlike prior work focusing on single-model emulation or physics-only schemes, it quantifies the benefits of multi-model diversity for robust generalization to truly unseen future and regional climates.

topic: Topological methods for high-dimensional data analysis
text:
IDEA: Integrating persistent spectral graph signatures derived from combinatorial Laplacian filtrations with Variational Autoencoder embeddings enhances clustering accuracy of heterogeneous single-cell RNA-seq datasets beyond either method alone.

BACKGROUND: Accurately clustering high-dimensional, heterogeneous biological data such as single-cell RNA-seq remains challenging due to complex nonlinear structures and subtle cell type distinctions. While neural network embeddings like VAEs capture nonlinear gene expression patterns, they may overlook robust topological and geometric features. Conversely, topological data analysis (TDA) methods reveal data shape but often lack expressive nonlinear representation power, limiting clustering performance when used independently.

APPROACH: We propose a novel integration pipeline that first constructs k-nearest neighbor graphs (k=30) from raw gene expression data and computes persistent spectral graph signatures via persistent combinatorial Laplacians across a multiscale filtration, extracting spectral summaries such as eigenvalue distributions and heat kernel signatures. These topological features are concatenated with low-dimensional latent embeddings generated by a VAE trained on the same data, producing augmented feature vectors that are input to Leiden community detection clustering. The fusion leverages the complementary strengths of topological robustness and nonlinear neural embeddings.

EXPERIMENT: We will evaluate clustering quality on the Human Cell Atlas bone marrow (Han et al., 2018) and Tabula Muris Senis (2020) scRNA-seq datasets using Adjusted Rand Index (ARI) and Normalized Mutual Information (NMI) against ground truth cell type labels. Baselines include VAE embeddings alone, persistent spectral graph features alone, TDA Mapper graphs, and UMAP embeddings followed by Leiden clustering. Statistical significance of improvements will be assessed via paired Wilcoxon tests over multiple random seeds, with ablation studies on k-NN parameters and filtration settings. Negative controls combining spectral features with PCA embeddings will verify integration specificity. Scalability protocols involving graph downsampling and spectral approximations will ensure computational feasibility.

NOVELTY: This work meaningfully advances prior TDA and neural embedding approaches by formally integrating persistent spectral graph signatures with deep generative embeddings for biological clustering, supported by rigorous controls and scalability strategies. Unlike previous methods focusing solely on Mapper graphs or single embedding types, our approach exploits persistent combinatorial Laplacians to capture multiscale topological and geometric information synergistically with neural latent spaces, providing a theoretically grounded and empirically validated framework for enhanced biological data analysis.

topic: Topological methods for high-dimensional data analysis
text:
IDEA: Integrating persistent spectral graph features derived from topological filtrations with neural network embeddings enhances classification accuracy and biological interpretability on high-dimensional single-cell RNA sequencing data beyond using either method alone.

BACKGROUND: Accurately classifying cell types from high-dimensional single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) data remains challenging due to complex biological heterogeneity and intrinsic data noise. While neural network embeddings such as variational autoencoders (VAEs) capture nonlinear transcriptomic variation, they often lack explicit topological structure representation. Recent advances in persistent spectral graph theory enable multiscale topological and geometric feature extraction, yet their complementary value alongside learned embeddings for biological classification remains underexplored.

APPROACH: We propose a hybrid model that concatenates persistent spectral graph features—computed from filtrations on k-nearest neighbor graphs of normalized gene expression profiles and summarized via eigenvalue-based descriptors of persistent combinatorial Laplacians—with 20-dimensional VAE latent embeddings. This composite feature vector is standardized and input into a downstream classifier (random forest or fully connected neural network) for cell type prediction. To ensure robustness, we incorporate rigorous nested cross-validation, hyperparameter tuning for k in kNN and latent dimension size, and include control baselines augmenting embeddings with random or conventional non-topological features to isolate the added value of topological summaries.

EXPERIMENT: We will evaluate the approach on two benchmark scRNA-seq datasets with ground truth labels: the 10x Genomics PBMC 3k dataset (~3,000 annotated immune cells) and the Mouse Brain Atlas transcriptomic dataset with neuronal subtype annotations. Classification accuracy and F1-score on a held-out 20% test split will measure performance. The hypothesis is supported if the integrated model statistically significantly (p < 0.05, paired bootstrap test) improves accuracy by at least 3 percentage points over the best individual baseline across datasets; failure to meet this criterion falsifies the hypothesis.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work focusing on either topological visualization or neural embeddings alone, this study formally integrates persistent spectral graph features with deep embeddings under a theoretically justified framework positing complementary, non-redundant representational information. It rigorously controls for confounding factors via feature augmentation baselines and nested validation, and critically assesses biological interpretability and stability of topological features in single-cell contexts, advancing topology-informed representation learning beyond conventional benchmarks.

topic: Topological methods for high-dimensional data analysis
text:
IDEA:  
Integrate time-varying persistent homology features computed via vineyard algorithms with temporal deep generative models (e.g., recurrent or convolutional VAEs) to more accurately capture dynamic changes in longitudinal high-dimensional biological data than static topological summaries or classical embeddings alone.

BACKGROUND:  
High-dimensional biological datasets such as longitudinal single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) and immune profiling reveal dynamic cellular processes that are challenging to characterize using static snapshots or conventional dimensionality reduction methods. While persistent homology has been applied to capture topological structure at single time points, existing approaches lack temporal continuity and cannot robustly represent evolving biological states. Addressing this, time-varying persistent homology provides a principled framework to track topological features continuously over time, but its utility in complex biological datasets remains unexplored.

APPROACH:  
We propose to compute vineyard-based time-varying persistence diagrams on longitudinal scRNA-seq and immune profiling datasets, encoding temporal topological trajectories as features input into temporal deep generative models such as recurrent VAEs (RVAEs) or Temporal Convolutional Network VAEs (TCN-VAEs). This joint modeling captures geometric, topological, and temporal dynamics within a unified latent representation. Rigorous preprocessing, noise stabilization, and dimensionality reduction steps will ensure computational feasibility and stability of vineyard computations on large noisy biological data.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate our method on the Mouse Embryonic Stem Cell Differentiation dataset (Mouse Cell Atlas, Han et al. 2018) and the COVID-19 longitudinal immune profiling dataset (Lucas et al. 2020). Performance will be assessed via predictive accuracy, F1 score, and AUC in forecasting future cell states or disease stages, and clustering quality measured by Adjusted Rand Index (ARI) and Normalized Mutual Information (NMI) for recovering known biological trajectories. Baselines include static persistent homology features aggregated per time point, classical dynamic embedding methods (Dynamic Time Warping + t-SNE/UMAP), and temporal deep generative models trained on non-topological features to isolate the added value of time-varying topological information. Statistical significance will be evaluated with bootstrapped tests requiring >5% absolute improvement (p < 0.05) in metrics.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely combines vineyard-based dynamic topological data analysis with temporal deep generative modeling to explicitly capture evolving shapes in biological data, overcoming limitations of static or snapshot-based TDA approaches. Unlike prior scRNA-seq or molecular dynamics studies using static or fuzzy topological summaries, our approach rigorously integrates temporal continuity and latent variable modeling, with detailed considerations of computational robustness and biological interpretability in noisy, high-dimensional, and temporally sampled datasets.

topic: Topological methods for high-dimensional data analysis
text:
IDEA: Integrate multi-scale persistent spectral graph features—derived from filtrations of combinatorial Laplacians—into deep neural networks to improve classification accuracy and robustness on noisy, high-dimensional single-cell RNA sequencing data beyond using topological or spectral features alone.

BACKGROUND: Classifying cell types from high-dimensional, noisy single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) data remains challenging due to biological and technical variability obscuring meaningful patterns. Traditional approaches either use raw gene expression or separately incorporate topological persistence or spectral graph features, but these methods fail to fully capture the intertwined geometric and topological structure of the data, limiting classification accuracy and robustness.

APPROACH: We propose constructing multi-scale filtrations of simplicial complexes from scRNA-seq data points and computing persistent combinatorial Laplacians to extract spectral features encoding both topological persistence and geometric shape information. These features are vectorized across multiple scales and concatenated with original gene expression profiles as inputs to a standardized deep neural network architecture, with rigorous ablation studies comparing persistence-only, spectral-only, and combined feature augmentations.

EXPERIMENT: We will benchmark classification accuracy and robustness on the 10x Genomics PBMC 3k and Allen Mouse Brain Atlas datasets, evaluating cell type prediction via cross-validated deep learning models. Performance gains will be statistically validated using paired bootstrap resampling (p < 0.05), with robustness assessed by accuracy degradation under synthetic Gaussian noise perturbations. Success requires the combined method to outperform the best single-augmentation baseline by at least 5% absolute accuracy on both datasets.

NOVELTY: This work uniquely bridges persistent homology and spectral graph theory by computing and leveraging multi-scale persistent combinatorial Laplacian spectra within deep neural classifiers, providing a theoretically grounded, noise-stable, and scalable framework that surpasses prior methods relying solely on either topological summaries or spectral features, thereby capturing richer intrinsic data structures for improved biomedical data classification.

topic: Topological methods for high-dimensional data analysis
text:
IDEA:  
Incorporating multi-parameter persistent homology that jointly analyzes gene expression similarity and functional annotation similarity improves cell type classification accuracy in scRNA-seq data beyond state-of-the-art Mapper and UMAP-based methods.

BACKGROUND:  
Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) data are intrinsically high-dimensional and heterogeneous, posing challenges for accurate cell type classification. Existing topological approaches such as Mapper capture shape but ignore functional gene annotations, while manifold learning methods like UMAP reduce dimensionality but may lose biologically relevant multi-modal information. Integrating functional annotations with topological features through multi-parameter persistent homology offers a promising framework to better capture complex cellular relationships.

APPROACH:  
We construct a bi-filtration on cells using two parameters: (1) gene expression similarity (e.g., correlation distance) and (2) functional annotation similarity derived from curated gene ontology or pathway enrichment profiles aggregated at the single-cell level. From this bi-filtration, we compute multi-parameter persistent homology modules and extract robust invariants such as rank invariants and generalized persistence landscapes, which serve as features for a gradient boosting classifier predicting cell types. To address scalability, we implement a scalable pipeline combining dimensionality reduction, landmark-based approximations, and parallelized persistence computations validated on benchmark datasets.

EXPERIMENT:  
We evaluate our method on the 10x Genomics 1.3 million mouse brain cells and PBMC 68k datasets, comparing classification accuracy, F1-scores, and confusion matrices against baselines including Mapper without annotations, UMAP+random forest, and single-parameter persistence on expression data alone. Statistical significance of at least 3% absolute accuracy improvement (p < 0.01 via bootstrapping) over the best baseline will support the hypothesis; ablation studies with randomized functional annotations will isolate the contribution of multi-parameter topology.

NOVELTY:  
This work is the first to rigorously integrate curated functional annotations at the single-cell level into a multi-parameter persistent homology framework for scRNA-seq, providing theoretical justification for bi-filtration stability and interpretability. It goes beyond prior Mapper or single-parameter persistence approaches by jointly encoding biological function and topology, and it pioneers scalable computational strategies for applying multi-parameter persistence to million-cell datasets.

topic: Quantum advantage in machine learning tasks
text:
IDEA:  
Quantum machine learning models employing variational quantum circuits trained on classical medical image datasets with over 30% class-imbalanced rare features can achieve statistically significant lower generalization error than matched classical models, particularly improving rare class recall and precision under balanced test conditions.

BACKGROUND:  
Class imbalance with rare but clinically critical features remains a major challenge for reliable medical image classification, where classical models often struggle to generalize well to minority classes. While quantum machine learning (QML) theoretically offers richer feature encoding capabilities, existing studies lack rigorous theoretical justification and fair empirical comparisons that isolate QML’s advantage in learning from rare, imbalanced data. This gap limits understanding of when and why QML might outperform classical methods on realistic clinical datasets.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hybrid quantum-classical pipeline combining a pretrained CNN feature extractor with an 8-qubit variational quantum circuit (VQC) classifier layer, trained end-to-end to classify medical image categories exhibiting controlled rare feature ratios (30-40%). Theoretical grounding from quantum learning theory suggests that quantum encoding induces a higher-dimensional Hilbert space representation that can better separate rare class features with sparse representation, motivating the chosen imbalance threshold and encoding strategy. Classical baselines include a ResNet-50 CNN and a classical classifier head matched in parameter count and architecture complexity to the quantum layer, ensuring a fair controlled comparison.

EXPERIMENT:  
We conduct stratified 5-fold cross-validation experiments on the ISIC 2018 skin lesion and Chest X-ray14 datasets, manipulating training data to maintain 30-40% rare class representation via realistic clinical augmentation and downsampling. Models are evaluated on two test sets: (1) balanced with equal rare/common classes to assess generalization to minority features, and (2) imbalanced reflecting natural distributions. The primary metric is generalization error (1 – balanced accuracy), with an emphasis on rare class recall and precision. Statistical significance of at least 5% absolute error reduction by QML over classical baselines on balanced test sets is assessed via paired t-tests (p < 0.05) to falsify or support the hypothesis.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely combines a theory-driven hypothesis grounded in quantum learning complexity with a rigorously controlled experimental design including parameter-matched classical baselines and clinically realistic data manipulations. Unlike prior studies focusing on generative tasks or software frameworks, it isolates and validates QML’s advantage in learning rare, imbalanced features in large-scale clinical image datasets through dual evaluation on balanced versus imbalanced test distributions, providing concrete evidence for or against quantum advantage in a critical real-world application domain.

topic: Quantum advantage in machine learning tasks
text:
IDEA:  
Quantum machine learning models leveraging variational circuits with amplitude-encoded low-rank PCA features will demonstrate statistically significant generalization improvements over state-of-the-art classical models in limited-data regimes on classical high-dimensional datasets with intrinsic low-rank structure.

BACKGROUND:  
Current evidence for quantum advantage in machine learning remains inconclusive, especially in realistic scenarios with classical high-dimensional data exhibiting low-rank structure. While classical dimension reduction and kernel methods efficiently exploit such structure, it remains an open question whether quantum feature maps and variational models can induce novel inductive biases that translate into measurable performance gains under limited training data. Addressing this gap is critical to justify near-term quantum machine learning efforts beyond classical algorithms optimized for low-rank data.

APPROACH:  
We propose to encode truncated PCA components—capturing low-rank subspaces of CIFAR-10, Fashion-MNIST, and Human Activity Recognition datasets—via amplitude encoding into variational quantum circuits designed with parameterized single-qubit rotations and entangling layers to form expressive quantum feature maps. Hybrid quantum-classical gradient-based training will optimize these models, while classical baselines—SVMs with RBF kernels, regularized deep neural networks, and kernel PCA classifiers—receive identical PCA-preprocessed inputs. Noise-aware simulations will be incorporated to assess robustness, and scalable circuit design heuristics will ensure computational feasibility.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate classification accuracy on held-out test sets across 10 randomized splits, subsampling training data at 5%, 10%, and 20% to simulate data-limited regimes. Statistical significance will be tested via paired t-tests, with the hypothesis supported if quantum models outperform the best classical baseline by at least 3% absolute accuracy with p < 0.01 at the smallest training sizes. Extensive hyperparameter tuning and noise sensitivity analyses will validate robustness and practical relevance.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely combines rigorous theoretical motivation linking quantum feature maps to low-rank subspace exploitation with comprehensive benchmarking against strong, noise-aware classical baselines specifically tailored to the same low-rank datasets. Unlike prior studies which focus on quantum generative models or general quantum kernels, our approach isolates quantum inductive biases arising from amplitude encoding of controlled-rank PCA features, providing the first falsifiable test of quantum advantage in classical data generalization under realistic constraints.

topic: Quantum advantage in machine learning tasks
text:
IDEA:  
Quantum machine learning models demonstrate a provable sample complexity advantage over classical models when learning probability distributions derived from quantum states with high entanglement entropy, tested via rigorously controlled benchmarks on synthetic and real quantum-correlated datasets.

BACKGROUND:  
Understanding whether and when quantum machine learning (QML) can outperform classical approaches in learning tasks remains an open challenge, particularly regarding the role of entanglement as a complexity measure. Prior work has not conclusively established if quantum correlations translate into practical sample efficiency gains, nor have experiments systematically isolated entanglement entropy’s effect on learnability under fair, noise-aware conditions. Clarifying this is critical for validating quantum advantage claims beyond asymptotic or computational complexity arguments.

APPROACH:  
We generate families of parameterized quantum states exhibiting tunable entanglement entropy using random quantum circuits inspired by the Google Sycamore architecture, supplemented by molecular electronic structure data from the OpenFermion Quantum Chemistry Dataset exhibiting physically meaningful entanglement. We then benchmark hybrid variational quantum classifiers and quantum generative models—trained via parameter-shift gradient methods—against classical variational autoencoders, deep neural networks, and kernel methods (Gaussian process regression, SVMs) on identical classical feature representations derived from quantum measurement outcomes. All models are capacity-normalized and hyperparameter-optimized with matched training budgets and noise models to ensure a fair assessment of sample complexity.

EXPERIMENT:  
We measure the minimum number of training samples \(N\) required for each model to reach fixed predictive performance thresholds (e.g., ≥95% classification accuracy or log-likelihood within 5% of optimal) on held-out test sets, across multiple controlled entanglement levels. Statistical hypothesis testing will validate whether \(N_{quantum} < \frac{1}{2} N_{best-classical}\) holds consistently. Classical models matching or exceeding quantum sample efficiency at all entanglement regimes will falsify the hypothesis.

NOVELTY:  
This work rigorously formalizes entanglement entropy as a complexity proxy linked to sample complexity gaps and implements standardized, noise-aware benchmarking protocols that control for model capacity and optimization fidelity—addressing a critical gap in existing empirical QML studies. By combining synthetic high-entanglement quantum data with realistic quantum-chemistry datasets and an explicit falsification criterion, it moves beyond prior exploratory demonstrations to provide a decisive test of quantum advantage in learning.

topic: Quantum advantage in machine learning tasks
text:
IDEA:  
Quantum machine learning models employing hybrid quantum-classical feature encoding and parameterized quantum circuits can achieve statistically significant lower generalization error than state-of-the-art classical models on high-dimensional, structured datasets within a fixed, realistic training time budget.

BACKGROUND:  
Demonstrating practical quantum advantage in machine learning remains an open challenge due to limitations such as barren plateaus, encoding overheads, and noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) hardware constraints. While classical deep learning excels on structured data like images and molecules, it is unclear whether hybrid quantum-classical models can leverage quantum feature spaces and entanglement to surpass classical generalization performance within realistic resource limits. Rigorous evaluation frameworks that incorporate realistic quantum hardware constraints and matched classical baselines are needed to validate potential advantages.

APPROACH:  
We propose hybrid quantum-classical architectures combining classical preprocessing (e.g., convolutional layers or graph embeddings) with parameterized quantum circuits (8-12 qubits, hardware-efficient ansatzes) designed to capture complex correlations via entanglement. Quantum data encoding uses optimized angle and graph-based feature maps tailored to preserve structural information. Models will be trained end-to-end using gradient-based optimization with parameter-shift rules and shot-noise-aware gradient estimation, incorporating error mitigation techniques and realistic noise models calibrated to superconducting or trapped-ion quantum devices. Classical baselines match quantum model parameter counts and include CNNs (ResNet-18 adapted for grayscale) and MPNNs for molecular regression.

EXPERIMENT:  
We evaluate on Fashion-MNIST (10-class grayscale image classification, test accuracy) and QM9 (HOMO-LUMO gap regression, mean absolute error), using fixed 24-hour wall-clock training time on equivalent classical and quantum-accelerated hardware. Success requires at least a 5% relative improvement in test accuracy or MAE reduction by the quantum model over classical baselines, with statistical significance confirmed via repeated runs (≥5) and two-sided t-tests at 95% confidence. Ablation studies isolate quantum encoding and circuit depth contributions under matched classical preprocessing.

NOVELTY:  
This work rigorously integrates realistic quantum hardware constraints and error mitigation, matching classical and quantum model capacity and preprocessing to isolate quantum effects, and provides complexity-motivated theoretical insights into quantum feature expressivity for structured data. Unlike prior empirical studies, it systematically quantifies shot-noise impacts and training resource scalability, offering a reproducible benchmark framework to decisively assess quantum advantage in practical machine learning tasks.

topic: Quantum advantage in machine learning tasks
text:
IDEA:  
Quantum machine learning models employing carefully matched quantum data encodings and hybrid variational circuits can achieve statistically significantly lower generalization error than classical CNN and SVM baselines on high-dimensional structured image datasets under limited training samples.

BACKGROUND:  
While quantum machine learning (QML) holds promise for outperforming classical models in complex tasks, definitive demonstrations of quantum advantage in practical learning scenarios remain elusive. A key open problem is whether QML can generalize better than classical methods from scarce, high-dimensional structured data, such as images, where classical models often require large datasets to avoid overfitting. Prior work has yet to conclusively address this question with rigorously matched model capacities, controlled encoding schemes, and statistically powered comparisons.

APPROACH:  
We develop hybrid quantum-classical classifiers in TensorFlow Quantum that encode 28x28 grayscale images into parameterized quantum circuits via dimension-controlled angle encoding on 4 to 6 qubits, followed by trainable variational layers and classical post-processing. Classical baselines—a CNN optimized for small data with capacity matched via controlled dimensionality reduction and an RBF-kernel SVM with tuned hyperparameters—are explicitly constrained to comparable representational complexity and input preprocessing to enable fair comparison of generalization behavior.

EXPERIMENT:  
We conduct 5-fold cross-validation on limited subsets (500 and 1000 training samples) of MNIST and Fashion-MNIST, comparing generalization error (test error minus training error) across models. Statistical significance of quantum advantage is assessed via paired t-tests with multiple hypothesis corrections, requiring at least a 2% absolute reduction in generalization error by the quantum model over both classical baselines to support the hypothesis.

NOVELTY:  
This work pioneers a rigorously controlled, statistically powered empirical test of quantum advantage in low-data, high-dimensional structured learning by explicitly matching data encodings and model capacities—a critical gap in prior QML studies. It also grounds the hypothesis within theoretical considerations of encoding expressivity and trainability, moving beyond exploratory or asymptotic claims to targeted, falsifiable evaluation of QML’s practical generalization benefits.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA: Implement tailored quantum error correction codes that leverage device-specific correlated noise patterns on superconducting NISQ processors to achieve at least a 30% reduction in logical error rates compared to standard generic QEC codes under identical hardware constraints.

BACKGROUND: Current NISQ-era superconducting processors exhibit complex, correlated noise sources that generic QEC codes do not fully exploit, limiting practical error suppression. Addressing the challenge of improving logical qubit fidelity within stringent qubit and gate-depth budgets requires error correction schemes specifically adapted to the dominant noise correlations observed in real devices. This approach aims to overcome the gap between generic QEC theory and the realities of NISQ hardware noise.

APPROACH: Using IBM Quantum Falcon processors’ publicly available, time-resolved noise characterization datasets (Qiskit Ignis), we construct detailed noise correlation matrices capturing gate errors, readout errors, and crosstalk. We then design customized QEC variants—such as modified surface or Bacon-Shor codes—with syndrome extraction circuits and decoders explicitly tailored to detect and mitigate dominant correlated error channels. The decoder incorporates correlated error likelihoods via a customized MWPM or belief-propagation algorithm, while syndrome extraction schedules are optimized at the pulse-level with Qiskit Pulse to minimize error propagation.

EXPERIMENT: We benchmark logical error rates over repeated logical state preparations and syndrome measurements on the same IBM Quantum Falcon device, comparing (1) the tailored QEC code exploiting device-specific noise correlations and (2) a baseline generic QEC code (standard surface or Steane code) matched for qubit count (<15 qubits) and gate-depth. Logical error rates are inferred via standard decoding and measured across multiple calibration cycles with interleaved benchmarking and measurement error mitigation. Success is defined as achieving ≥30% logical error rate reduction averaged over noise realizations; failure is defined as <10% improvement or degradation.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that applies generic QEC or focuses on idealized noise models, this study explicitly integrates real-time device noise correlations into both code and decoder design, using pulse-level hardware-aware scheduling to mitigate correlated errors in a realistic NISQ setting. The ≥30% logical error reduction target is theoretically justified by linking noise correlation strength to error suppression bounds, representing a concrete, experimentally validated advance beyond existing benchmarks on superconducting hardware.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA: Implementing an adaptive quantum error correction protocol that dynamically adjusts syndrome extraction frequency based on real-time Bayesian noise profiling will reduce logical error rates by at least 30% compared to fixed-frequency schedules in NISQ superconducting qubit devices.

BACKGROUND: NISQ-era quantum processors suffer from time-varying and heterogeneous noise that deteriorates logical qubit fidelity, yet standard QEC schedules use static syndrome extraction intervals that do not exploit real-time noise fluctuations. Efficiently balancing syndrome measurement overhead and error suppression remains an open challenge under realistic hardware latency and control constraints. Demonstrating practical adaptive QEC strategies tailored for current superconducting platforms can enable more effective error mitigation within limited coherence times.

APPROACH: We propose a real-time adaptive QEC protocol employing Bayesian or Kalman filtering of syndrome data to estimate instantaneous noise parameters (e.g., T1 and T2 fluctuations) on IBM and Rigetti superconducting qubit backends. These estimates inform a hysteresis-controlled policy that increases syndrome extraction frequency during high-noise periods and reduces it when noise subsides, thus minimizing measurement-induced errors and overhead. Error decoding is performed using minimum-weight perfect matching algorithms adapted for variable syndrome densities to accurately track logical errors.

EXPERIMENT: Using the Quantum Error Correction Benchmarking (QECB) suite’s calibrated noise models and logical error per cycle (LEPC) metrics for [[5,1,3]] and surface codes, we will compare the adaptive protocol against matched fixed-frequency baselines with identical total syndrome extraction counts. Experiments will run on IBM Quantum Experience devices with Qiskit Pulse to implement real-time classical feedback for syndrome timing control, performing 1000 runs per noise regime to statistically validate at least 30% LEPC reduction (p < 0.05) or falsify the hypothesis if improvement is not observed.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work focused on static QEC schedules or theoretical noise modeling, this study rigorously integrates real-time Bayesian noise estimation with dynamic control and decoding on real superconducting hardware under realistic latency constraints. It explicitly quantifies the tradeoff between syndrome measurement overhead and error suppression, providing the first experimentally grounded demonstration of adaptive QEC’s practical value in NISQ devices.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA:  
Implementing a supervised recurrent neural network (RNN)-based adaptive, real-time syndrome extraction protocol with low-latency FPGA feedback on IBM Falcon NISQ devices can reduce logical error rates in Surface-17 quantum error correction codes by at least 20% compared to static and heuristic adaptive syndrome measurement schedules.

BACKGROUND:  
Current NISQ quantum error correction (QEC) demonstrations predominantly rely on static syndrome measurement schedules, limiting their ability to respond dynamically to evolving error patterns and noise fluctuations. Adaptive syndrome extraction protocols without machine learning (ML) feedback provide modest benefits but do not leverage data-driven optimization of measurement sequences in real time. There remains a critical need to integrate low-latency ML-guided feedback directly into quantum control hardware to improve error detection efficiency and reduce logical error rates under realistic device noise and drift conditions.

APPROACH:  
We propose training a supervised RNN model offline on labeled syndrome measurement patterns and logical error outcomes from the Qiskit Surface-17 QEC dataset, capturing temporal correlations and error propagation dynamics. This model will then be deployed via FPGA co-processors integrated with IBM Falcon device control electronics to perform real-time inference and guide adaptive selection of syndrome measurements during live experiments. The adaptive protocol optimizes measurement allocation each cycle based on prior syndrome outcomes and error likelihoods, reducing unnecessary syndrome extraction overhead and improving logical qubit fidelity.

EXPERIMENT:  
Logical error rates will be evaluated over 10,000 syndrome extraction cycles per run, averaged across at least 50 independent repetitions on IBM Falcon processors using Qiskit Pulse control, comparing (1) the static syndrome schedule baseline from prior full-stack studies, (2) heuristic adaptive syndrome extraction, and (3) the ML-guided adaptive protocol. In-situ calibration and drift tracking will be incorporated to ensure ML model validity under real-time noise conditions. Statistical significance (p < 0.05) of a ≥20% reduction in logical error rates for the ML approach over static baseline will confirm the hypothesis; failure to achieve this or to outperform heuristic adaptation will falsify it.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely integrates a low-latency, FPGA-accelerated supervised RNN for real-time syndrome extraction adaptation directly within the quantum control stack of a leading superconducting NISQ platform—going beyond prior adaptive schemes that lack ML feedback or real-time hardware integration. It also rigorously incorporates live calibration and control experiments to validate ML model performance under realistic noise dynamics, providing a concrete pathway toward practical, data-driven QEC scheduling in current devices.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA:  
Implementing hardware-aware, noise-tailored approximate quantum error correction codes on superconducting NISQ devices can increase logical qubit coherence times by at least 50% compared to standard repetition codes under matched noise and experimental conditions.

BACKGROUND:  
Current NISQ devices suffer from complex, non-symmetric noise that limits logical qubit performance, and standard QEC codes like repetition codes are suboptimal under such realistic noise. While approximate and noise-adaptive QEC schemes have been proposed theoretically, their practical efficacy and robustness on real hardware with time-varying, non-Markovian noise remain insufficiently explored. Demonstrating substantial logical coherence improvements with implementable tailored codes is an open challenge critical for extending NISQ device capabilities.

APPROACH:  
We design approximate QEC codes optimized via variational recovery adapted to the dominant, device-specific noise channels (e.g., amplitude damping and dephasing) extracted from IBM Quantum’s Falcon processors’ calibration data. The codes leverage hardware-aware syndrome extraction and circuit compilation to minimize depth and error propagation, with explicit error mitigation and real-time noise monitoring protocols integrated to stabilize benchmarking. Logical coherence times \(T_L\) are compared against those from the baseline 3-qubit repetition code and unencoded physical qubits to quantify improvements.

EXPERIMENT:  
Using IBM Quantum Experience calibration data and randomized benchmarking sequences with syndrome measurement and recovery implemented on ibmq_casablanca, we extract logical decay curves and fit \(T_L\) for both codes under identical noise conditions, including unencoded controls. Success is defined as achieving \(T_L \geq 1.5 \times T_L^{rep}\) with statistical significance across multiple runs and noise calibrations, thereby testing the hypothesis under realistic, variable noise.

NOVELTY:  
This work goes beyond prior approximate QEC demonstrations by combining noise-tailored variational recovery with hardware-aware circuit optimizations and robust experimental controls that explicitly address time-dependent and non-Markovian noise in superconducting NISQ hardware. It establishes a concrete, reproducible benchmark for logical coherence gains feasible in near-term quantum processors, bridging theory and practice.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA:  
Implement a hybrid quantum error correction (QEC) protocol that dynamically switches in real time between erasure conversion and standard stabilizer code decoding based on sliding-window syndrome error statistics to reduce logical error rates on NISQ superconducting and Rydberg devices by at least 30% compared to static QEC methods.

BACKGROUND:  
NISQ devices suffer from diverse and fluctuating error sources, including leakage and erasure errors, limiting the effectiveness of static QEC protocols. While erasure conversion improves decoding by explicitly identifying leakage errors, static approaches fail to adapt to temporal error rate variations, reducing error correction efficacy. Real-time adaptive switching between error correction modes could better match current error profiles and improve logical qubit fidelity, but its practical feasibility and advantage on existing hardware remain unproven.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hybrid QEC scheme that continuously analyzes syndrome measurement outcomes over a sliding window of 10 cycles, estimating leakage/erasure error rates to decide on-the-fly whether to apply standard MWPM stabilizer decoding or erasure-aware MWPM decoding. The switching threshold (e.g., 5% erasure rate) and window size will be optimized offline using Google Quantum AI’s Surface-17 syndrome and logical error data combined with erasure profiles from the 2023 Science Rydberg erasure conversion study. The protocol will be implemented with pulse-level control on IBM’s Falcon superconducting qubits (ibmq_montreal) and simulated on Rigetti’s Aspen-11 Rydberg platform to ensure hardware compatibility and realistic noise integration.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will benchmark three protocols—(a) static standard Surface-17 MWPM decoding, (b) static erasure conversion with erasure-aware decoding, and (c) the proposed hybrid dynamic switching protocol—running 1000 trials each with realistic injected noise profiles and native device noise. Logical error rates per QEC cycle, decoding latency (to remain under 2× static baselines), and physical qubit overhead (≤1.5× static codes) will be measured. The hypothesis is confirmed if the hybrid protocol reduces logical error rates by ≥30% relative to the best static baseline with statistically significant confidence; otherwise, it is falsified.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely integrates real-time syndrome-driven adaptive decoder switching on NISQ hardware with explicit pulse-level erasure detection, bridging experimental feasibility gaps left by prior static or continuously adaptive decoders. Unlike existing approaches, it rigorously quantifies latency overhead, hardware constraints, and error model realism, establishing practical thresholds for dynamic QEC benefits in contemporary quantum processors.
